blocks.html:
EXPOSĂ‰ V
GENERALITIES ON \(\lambda\)-RINGSby P. Berthelot

The theory of \(\lambda\)-rings is useful whenever one uses rings such as the ring of classes of vector bundles on a ringed site, the ring of representations of a group, etc..., for which the exterior power operations define maps \(\lambda^i\). We shall use it extensively in the rest of the Seminar, so it is necessary first to develop its general formalism. This exposĂ© is therefore entirely independent of the rest of the seminar and can serve users of \(\lambda\)-rings outside algebraic geometry.

The first paragraph essentially gives the theory of "multiplicative sequences of polynomials" of Hirzebruch (cf. [2]); the functorial point of view adopted here has the advantage, besides its generality, of considerably reducing the computations. The following paragraphs will show how, to every augmented \(\lambda\)-ring, one can associate a "Chern ring" allowing one to develop a theory of Chern classes. It is this theory which will allow us to state the Riemann-Roch theorem in the general form of Exp. VIII.
1. Universal polynomials

Let \(K\) be a commutative ring with unit element, fixed throughout this paragraph. We denote by \(\mathcal C\) the category of filtered \(K\)-algebras, commutative with unit element, whose filtration is decreasing and such that the group of elements of filtration zero is the whole algebra. We define a functor on \(\mathcal C\), with values in the category of abelian groups, in the following way: if \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\), we associate to \(A\) the set of formal power series \(\psi\in A[[t]]\) satisfying the following conditions: if \(\psi=a_0+a_1t+\cdots+a_nt^n+\cdots\), then
\[(1.1.1)\qquad a_0=1\ ;\quad \forall i\geq1,\ a_i\in\operatorname{Fil}^iA.\]

This set will be denoted \(\widehat G_K(A)\), or \(\widehat G(A)\) if no confusion is possible; it is clear that it is an abelian group under multiplication of formal power series, and that in this way we indeed define a functor
\[\widehat G_K:K\text{-}\underline{\mathrm{Alg.fil.}}\longrightarrow\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}.\]
If \(A\) is endowed with the coarse filtration, \(\widehat G(A)\) is the group of formal power series with augmentation 1, which we shall also denote \(1+A[[t]]^+\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.

The functor \(\widehat G_K\) is representable.

Let \((X_i)_{i\in\mathbb N,\,i\geq1}\) be a family of indeterminates; we assign the weight \(i\) to \(X_i\), and we filter the ring \(K[X_i]_{i\geq1}\) by the weight with respect to the \(X_i\). We then define a bijection:
\[\widehat G_K(A)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{K\text{-}\mathrm{Alg.fil.}}(K[X_i]_{i\geq1},A)\]

by associating to every series \(\psi=1+a_1t+\cdots+a_nt^n+\cdots\in\widehat G_K(A)\) the homomorphism \(f_\psi\) determined by \(f_\psi(X_i)=a_i\). This homomorphism is compatible with the filtrations, and we thus obtain an isomorphism of functors.

We set \(\varphi=1+X_1t+\cdots+X_nt^n+\cdots\); for every filtered \(K\)-algebra \(A\) and every series \(\psi\in\widehat G(A)\), there exists one and only one homomorphism \(f_\psi\) from \(K[X_i]_{i\geq1}\) to \(A\) such that \(\widehat G(f_\psi)(\varphi)=\psi\).

We define for every \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\) a filtration on \(\widehat G(A)\) in the following way: a series \(\psi=1+a_1t+\cdots\) will be said to be of filtration \(\geq k\) if \(a_i=0\) for \(1\leq i<k\). We denote by \(F^k\widehat G(A)\) the subgroup thus obtained; it is a functor on \(\mathcal C\), and we shall henceforth regard \(\widehat G\) as a functor with values in the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}\) of filtered abelian groups by means of this filtration (with \(F^0\widehat G=\widehat G\)).
Let \(H\) be a functor on \(\mathcal C\) with values in \(\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}\); we shall suppose that \(H\) satisfies the two following conditions:
i) \(\forall A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\), \(H(A)\) is separated and complete.

ii) If \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\) is a graded \(K\)-algebra, endowed with the filtration associated to its graduation, and if \(G\) is a finite group acting on \(A\) by graded morphisms, then \(H(A^G)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}H(A)^G\) (the exponent \(G\) denoting the invariants under \(G\)).
In fact, we shall need these conditions only when \(A\) is a polynomial ring, and \(G\) a finite product of symmetric groups acting by permutation of the indeterminates.
Examples: 1) \(H(A)=\widehat G(A)\).
2) \(H(A)=1+A[[t]]^+\); this functor will be denoted \(\widehat G_g\) (\(g\), the initial of "grossiĂ¨re").

3) \(H(A)=\widehat G_a(A)\), where \(\widehat G_a(A)\) denotes the group (under addition) of formal power series such that \(a_0=0\), \(a_i\in\operatorname{Fil}^iA\) for \(i\geq1\), endowed with the filtration analogous to that of \(\widehat G\).

For every \(n\), we shall denote by \(f_n\) the endomorphism of \(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\) defined by \(f_n(X_i)=X_i\) if \(i<n\), \(f_n(X_n)=0\). It factors through the inclusion of \(K[X_1,\ldots,X_{n-1}]\) in \(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\), which shows that the homomorphism from \(H(K[X_1,\ldots,X_{n-1}])\) to \(H(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n])\) is injective, and will allow us to identify the former with a subgroup of the latter, and likewise we shall regard \(H(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})\) as contained in \(H(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})\).
We note that \(f_1\) is none other than the augmentation of \(K[X]\).

We embed \(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\) in \(K[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\) by sending \(X_i\) to the \(i\)-th symmetric function of the \(T_j\); by letting the symmetric group \(\mathfrak S_n\) act by permutation of the \(T_j\), and using the theorem on symmetric polynomials and (1.3.ii), we see that the homomorphism from \(H(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n])\) to \(H(K[T_1,\ldots,T_n])\) deduced from it is injective, which will again allow us to identify the former with a subgroup of the latter:
\[H(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n])\simeq H(K[T_1,\ldots,T_n])^{\mathfrak S_n}lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow H(K[T_1,\ldots,T_n]).\]
We denote by
\[u_i^n:K[X]\longrightarrow K[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\]
the homomorphism defined by \(u_i^n(X)=T_i\), and we set
\[u_H^n=\sum_{i=1}^n H(u_i^n):H(K[X])\longrightarrow H(K[T_1,\ldots,T_n]).\]
The symmetric group permutes the morphisms \(H(u_i^n)\), and therefore leaves invariant the morphism \(u_H^n\), which consequently takes its values in
Let us finally remark that if \(H=\widehat G\),
\[u_H^n(1+Xt)=\prod_{i=1}^n(1+T_it)=1+X_1t+\cdots+X_nt^n.\]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.6.

The map from \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}}(\widehat G_K,H)\) to \(H(K[X])\) which, to a morphism of functors, associates its value on the element \(1+Xt\) of \(\widehat G_K(K[X])\), is injective; its image is the subgroup of \(H(K[X])\) formed of the elements \(\theta\) such that (with the notations of 1.4 for the \(f_n\)):

i) \(H(f_1)(\theta)=0\).

ii) \(\forall n\geq1\), \(H(f_n)(u_H^n(\theta))=u_H^n(\theta)\pmod {F^nH(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n])}\).

Let us first show the injectivity. By Lemma 1.2, it suffices to see that a morphism of functors \(\Phi\) which is zero on \(1+Xt\) is zero on \(\varphi=1+X_1t+\cdots+X_nt^n+\cdots\). The commutative diagram:
\[(1.6.1)\qquad \xymatrix{ \widehat G(K[X])\ar[r]^{u_{\widehat G}^n}\ar[d]_{\Phi}& \widehat G(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n])\ar[d]^{\Phi}\\ H(K[X])\ar[r]_{u_H^n}&H(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]) }\]
shows that for every \(n\), \(\Phi(1+X_1t+\cdots+X_nt^n)=0\).

Now \(\varphi=1+X_1t+\cdots+X_nt^n\pmod {F^{n+1}\widehat G}\); hence \(\Phi(\varphi)=\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)\pmod {F^{n+1}H}\), and consequently \(\Phi(\varphi)=0\pmod {F^{n+1}H}\), for every \(n\); since \(H(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})\) is separated and complete, \(\Phi(\varphi)=0\).
Now let \(\Phi\) be an arbitrary element of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G,H)\), and let \(\theta=\Phi(1+Xt)\); let us show that \(\theta\) satisfies conditions i) and ii).
i): \(H(f_1)(\theta)=H(f_1)\circ\Phi(1+Xt)=\Phi\circ\widehat G(f_1)(1+Xt)=\Phi(1)=0\).

ii): \(u_H^n(\theta)=\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)\) by (1.6.1); now \(1+\cdots+X_nt^n=1+\cdots+X_{n-1}t^{n-1}\pmod {F^n\widehat G}\), and \(1+\cdots+X_{n-1}t^{n-1}=\widehat G(f_n)(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)\). Since \(\Phi\circ\widehat G(f_n)=H(f_n)\circ\Phi\), and since \(\Phi\) is a morphism of filtered functors, we obtain ii).
Finally, we must show that if \(\theta\in H(K[X])\) satisfies i) and ii), there exists \(\Phi\in\operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G,H)\) such that \(\theta=\Phi(1+Xt)\).
We first determine the value of \(\Phi\) for the element \(1+\cdots+X_nt^n\) of \(\widehat G(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n])\) by means of (1.6.1); we therefore have by definition:
\[\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)=\sum_{i=1}^n\Phi(1+T_it),\]
the \(\Phi(1+T_it)\) being known by functoriality by means of the morphisms \(u_i^n\).
We then glue the \(\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)\) for varying \(n\) by means of the commutative diagram:
\[(1.6.2)\qquad \xymatrix{ K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\ar[r]^{f_n}\ar[d]_{\sigma_n}&K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\ar[d]^{\sigma'_n}\\ K[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\ar[r]_{f'_n}&K[T_1,\ldots,T_n], }\]

where \(f'_n\) is determined by \(f'_n(T_i)=T_i\) if \(i<n\), \(f'_n(T_n)=0\), where \(\sigma_n\) sends \(X_i\) to the \(i\)-th symmetric function of the \(T_j\), and \(\sigma'_n\) sends \(X_i\) to the \(i\)-th symmetric function of \(T_1,\ldots,T_{n-1}\) for \(i<n\), and \(X_n\) to 0.
Indeed, we deduce from it:

\[ \begin{aligned} H(f_n)(\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)) &=H(f'_n)\left(\sum_{i=1}^n\Phi(1+T_it)\right)\\ &=\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\Phi(1+T_it)\quad\text{(par 1.6.i)}\\ &=\Phi(1+\cdots+X_{n-1}t^{n-1}) \end{aligned} \]
(placing ourselves in \(H(K[T_1,\ldots,T_{n-1}])\) by means of the identifications 1.4).

Taking 1.6.ii) into account, \(\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)=\Phi(1+\cdots+X_{n-1}t^{n-1})\pmod {F^nH(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})}\), and the \(\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)\) define an element \(\psi\) of \(H(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})\) since it is a separated complete group; we shall therefore set \(\Phi(\varphi)=\psi\).

Finally, let \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\), and \(\chi\in\widehat G(A)\); we set \(\Phi(\chi)=H(f_\chi)(\psi)\), where \(f_\chi\) is the homomorphism from \(K[X_i]_{i\geq1}\) to \(A\) defined in 1.2. Since we have already defined \(\Phi\) on the elements \(1+\cdots+X_nt^n=\varphi_n\), we must check that the two definitions give the same thing, that is to say that:
\[H(f_{\varphi_n})(\psi)=\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n).\]
Now for every \(p\), we have: \(\psi\equiv\Phi(1+\cdots+X_p t^p)\pmod {F^{p+1}H(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})}\). Whence for every \(p\geq n\);
\[H(f_{\varphi_n})(\psi)\equiv H(f_{\varphi_n})(\Phi(1+\cdots+X_p t^p))\pmod {F^{p+1}H(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})}.\]
Using the identifications of 1.4 and the glueing of the \(\Phi(\varphi_m)\) for varying \(m\), we obtain: \(H(f_{\varphi_n})(\Phi(\varphi_p))=\Phi(\varphi_n)\). We therefore finally have:
\[\forall p\geq n,\quad H(f_{\varphi_n})(\psi)\equiv\Phi(1+\cdots+X_nt^n)\pmod {F^pH(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})}.\]
Since \(H\) is separated, we deduce from this the desired equality.

We have thus defined a system of maps \(\Phi_A:\widehat G(A)\longrightarrow H(A)\); to check that they constitute a morphism of functors (with values in \(\underline{\mathrm{Ens}}\)), we must show that for every morphism \(h:A\longrightarrow A'\), the diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{ \widehat G(A)\ar[r]^{\widehat G(h)}\ar[d]_{\Phi_A}&\widehat G(A')\ar[d]^{\Phi_{A'}}\\ H(A)\ar[r]_{H(h)}&H(A') } \]
is commutative. Let \(\chi\in\widehat G(A)\); then: \(h\circ f_\chi=f_{\widehat G(h)(\chi)}\); whence:
\[H(h)(\Phi_A(\chi))=H(h)\circ H(f_\chi)(\psi)=H(f_{\widehat G(h)(\chi)})(\psi)=\Phi_{A'}(\widehat G(h)(\chi)).\]
It therefore remains to show that the morphism of functors thus constructed is compatible with the structures of filtered groups.

Suppose that \(\chi\in F^n\widehat G(A)\). To say that \(\chi\) is of filtration \(\geq n\) amounts to saying that \(f_\chi:K[X_i]_{i\geq1}\longrightarrow A\) factors through the endomorphism \(g_n\) of \(K[X_i]_{i\geq1}\) defined by: \(g_n(X_i)=0\) if \(1\leq i\leq n-1\), and \(g_n(X_i)=X_i\) if \(i\geq n\). To show that \(\Phi\) is compatible with the filtrations, it therefore suffices to show that \(H(g_n)(\Phi(\varphi))\in F^nH\). Now by construction:
\[\Phi(\varphi)\equiv\Phi(1+\cdots+X_{n-1}t^{n-1})\pmod {F^nH},\]
whence:
\[H(g_n)(\Phi(\varphi))\equiv H(g_n)(\Phi(1+\cdots+X_{n-1}t^{n-1}))=0\pmod {F^nH}.\]

To check the additivity of \(\Phi\), it suffices to do so for the elements of finite degree of \(\widehat G(A)\), and then to pass to the limit. So let \(\chi_1,\chi_2\) be two elements of degree \(n\) of \(\widehat G(A)\), and \(\chi\) their product. We consider the commutative diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{ K[X_1,\ldots,X_{2n}]\ar[r]^{f_\chi}\ar[d]&A\\ (K[T_1,\ldots,T_{2n}])^{\mathfrak S_n\times\mathfrak S_n}\ar[ur]_{f'_\chi}& } \]
where the arrows are defined as follows: \(\mathfrak S_n\times\mathfrak S_n\) acts on \(K[T_1,\ldots,T_{2n}]\)

in such a way that the first factor permutes \(T_1,\ldots,T_n\) and the second \(T_{n+1},\ldots,T_{2n}\); \(K[X_1,\ldots,X_{2n}]\) is embedded in the ring of invariants by sending \(X_j\) to the \(j\)-th symmetric function of the \(T_i\); \(f'_\chi\) sends the \(i\)-th symmetric function of \(T_1,\ldots,T_n\) (resp. \(T_{n+1},\ldots,T_{2n}\)) to the \(i\)-th coefficient of \(\chi_1\) (resp. \(\chi_2\)). Then:
\[ \Phi(\chi)=H(f_\chi)\left(\Phi\left(\prod_{i=1}^{2n}(1+T_it)\right)\right) =H(f'_\chi)\left(\Phi\left(\prod_{i=1}^{2n}(1+T_it)\right)\right); \]
\[ \Phi(\chi_1)=H(f'_\chi)\left(\Phi\left(\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1+T_it)\right)\right) \quad(\text{resp. }\chi_2,\ i=n+1,\ldots,2n). \]

Since \(H(f'_\chi)\) is a group homomorphism, we are reduced to the case where \(\chi=\prod_{i=1}^{2n}(1+T_it)\), and in this case the additivity of \(\Phi\) follows from the definition of the \(\Phi(1+X_1t+\cdots+X_kt^k)\).

We can generalize the preceding considerations to the case of a product of functors \(\widehat G\); the functor \((\widehat G)^k\) is representable by the ring \(K[(X_i^{(j)})]\), \(j=1,\ldots,k\), and \(i\geq1\), where \(X_i^{(j)}\) is of weight \(i\). We shall denote by \(\varphi^{(j)}\) the series \(1+X_1^{(j)}t+\cdots+X_n^{(j)}t^n+\cdots\); the element \((\varphi^{(j)})_j\) of \((\widehat G(K[(X_i^{(j)})_j]))^k\) possesses the universal property analogous to that of \(\varphi\) (cf. 1.2).

We can introduce the morphisms \(f_n^{(j)}\), \(u_H^{n,(j)}\) as before by redoing the construction for each family \((X_i^{(j)})_{i\geq1}\) separately. We obtain a result analogous to Theorem 1.6 by looking for the homomorphisms of functors from \((\widehat G)^k\) to \(H\) which are \(\mathbb Z\)-multilinear and compatible with the filtrations with respect to each of the \(k\) arguments separately: they are determined by their value for the element \((1+X^{(j)}t)_{j=1,\ldots,k}\) of \((\widehat G(K[(X^{(j)})_j]))^k\); an element \(\theta\) of \(H(K[(X^{(j)})_j])\) determines a morphism of functors if and only if it satisfies:
ii) \(\forall n,j\), \(H(f_n^{(j)})(u_H^{n,(j)}(\theta))=u_H^{n,(j)}(\theta)\pmod {F^nH}\).
The reader will easily convince himself that, up to complications of notation, it suffices to copy the proof of 1.6.
We shall denote by \(\mathcal C_0\) the category of \(K\)-algebras, and by
\[i:\mathcal C_0\longrightarrow\mathcal C,\]
the functor which to a \(K\)-algebra \(A\) associates the \(K\)-algebra \(A\) endowed with the coarse filtration; we shall denote by
\[g:\mathcal C\longrightarrow\mathcal C_0,\]
the "forgetting the filtration" functor. These functors satisfy the following relations:
\[g\circ i=\operatorname{Id}_{\mathcal C_0}\ ;\qquad \widehat G\circ i\circ g=\widehat G_g\ ;\qquad \widehat G_g\circ i\circ g=\widehat G_g\quad(\text{cf. 1.3, ex. 2}).\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.9.

Let \(H:\mathcal C_0\longrightarrow\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}\) be a functor on \(\mathcal C_0\); then the canonical homomorphism:

\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}}(\widehat G,H\circ g) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}}(\widehat G\circ i,H)\]

is an isomorphism.
To see that it is injective, we use the sequence of canonical homomorphisms:

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G,H\circ g)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G\circ i,H)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G_g,H\circ g)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G,H\circ g), \]

the last arrow coming from the fact that \(\widehat G\) is a subfunctor of \(\widehat G_g\). To check that the composite homomorphism is the identity, we remark that a morphism of functors: \(\widehat G\longrightarrow H\circ g\) is determined by its value for the objects of \(\mathcal C\) whose filtration is coarse; indeed, let \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\), and let \(A_g\) be the algebra \(A\) endowed with the coarse filtration; the canonical morphism \(A\longrightarrow A_g\) defines a commutative diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{ \widehat G(A)\ar[r]^{\Phi_A}\ar[d]&H\circ g(A)\ar[d]\\ \widehat G(A_g)\ar[r]_{\Phi_{A_g}}&H\circ g(A_g), } \]
showing that \(\Phi_A\) is the restriction of \(\Phi_{A_g}\) to \(\widehat G(A)\).
We then consider the composite homomorphism:

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G\circ i,H)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G_g,H\circ g)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G,H\circ g)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G\circ i,H), \]
and it is clear that it is the identity, whence the announced result.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.10.

Let \(H\) be a functor on the category \(\mathcal C_0\) of \(K\)-algebras, with values in the category of separated complete filtered abelian groups, and commuting with invariants under a finite group. Then the map from \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}}(\widehat G\circ i,H)\) to \(H(K[X])\) which, to a morphism of functors, associates its value on the element \(1+Xt\) of \(\widehat G\circ i(K[X])\), is injective; its image is the subgroup of \(H(K[X])\) formed of the elements \(\theta\) such that (with notations analogous to those of 1.4 and 1.5):

i) \(H(f_1)(\theta)=0\);

ii) \(\forall n\geq1\), \(H(f_n)(u_H^n(\theta))\equiv u_H^n(\theta)\pmod {F^nH(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n])}\).
The theorem follows immediately from Theorem 1.6 and Lemma 1.9, taking into account the fact that \(H\circ g(K[X])\) does not depend on the filtration chosen on \(K[X]\).
The analogous theorem obtained by replacing \(\widehat G\) with \((\widehat G)^k\) is also true (cf. 1.7).

When there is no risk of confusion, we shall denote the functor \(\widehat G\circ i\) by \(\widehat G\), which is legitimate, since \(i\) identifies \(\mathcal C_0\) with a subcategory of \(\mathcal C\).

We shall denote by \(\mathcal C_g\) the category of graded \(K\)-algebras, and by \(j\) the functor from \(\mathcal C_g\) to \(\mathcal C\) which to a graded \(K\)-algebra \(B\) associates the \(K\)-algebra \(B\) endowed with the filtration canonically defined by its graduation; the "associated graded" functor: \(\mathcal C\longrightarrow\mathcal C_g\), will be denoted \(\operatorname{Gr}\). There exists an isomorphism of functors \(\varepsilon:\operatorname{Id}_{\mathcal C_g}\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Gr}\circ j\).

Let \(B\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C_g)\); we shall denote by \(1+\widehat B^+\) the multiplicative group of the elements of \(\prod_{i=0}^\infty B^i\) with augmentation 1. By endowing it with the filtration analogous to that of \(\widehat G\), we thus define a functor: \(\mathcal C_g\longrightarrow\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}\).

Moreover, we define for every \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\) a homomorphism of filtered groups: \(\widehat G(A)\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}A}^+\), by associating to the series \(1+\cdots+a_nt^n+\cdots\),
the series \(1+\cdots+\bar a_n+\cdots\), where \(\bar a_n\) is the class of \(a_n\) in \(\operatorname{Gr}^nA\). Its kernel is the group \(\widehat G'(A)\) of formal power series such that:
\[a_0=1\ ;\quad\forall i\geq1,\ a_i\in\operatorname{Fil}^{i+1}A\ ;\]
we therefore have an isomorphism, functorial in \(A\):
\[\widehat G(A)/\widehat G'(A)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}A}^+.\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.12.

Let \(H:\mathcal C_g\longrightarrow\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}\) be a functor on \(\mathcal C_g\); then the canonical homomorphism:

\[\rho:\operatorname{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}}(1+\widehat{\ }^+,H) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}}(\widehat G,H\circ\operatorname{Gr})\]

is an isomorphism.
The homomorphism \(\rho\) is defined as the composite:

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(1+\widehat{\ }^+,H)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+,H\circ\operatorname{Gr})\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G,H\circ\operatorname{Gr}), \]
the last arrow being defined by the homomorphism \(\widehat G\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+\).
We shall construct a morphism in the opposite direction:
\[\rho':\operatorname{Hom}(\widehat G,H\circ\operatorname{Gr})\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(1+\widehat{\ }^+,H).\]

For this, we shall show that every morphism of functors from \(\widehat G\) to \(H\circ\operatorname{Gr}\) factors through the canonical morphism \(\widehat G\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+\), which is equivalent to showing that it vanishes on \(\widehat G'\) by 1.11.

Let \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C)\), \(\chi\in\widehat G'(A)\); we define a homomorphism of filtered \(K\)-algebras \(K[X_i]_{i\geq1}\longrightarrow A\) by sending \(X_1\) to 0 and \(X_i\) to the \((i-1)\)-th coefficient of \(\chi\) for \(i>1\); using this homomorphism, we see that it suffices to check that the morphism of functors under consideration vanishes for the element \(1+X_2t+\cdots+X_{n+1}t^n+\cdots\). We then use the endomorphism \(f\) of \(K[X_i]_{i\geq1}\) defined by \(f(X_i)=X_{i+1}\) for every \(i\), and the commutative diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{ \widehat G(K[(X_i)])\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\widehat G(f)}&H\circ\operatorname{Gr}(K[(X_i)])\ar[d]^{H(\operatorname{Gr}(f))}\\ \widehat G(K[(X_i)])\ar[r]&H\circ\operatorname{Gr}(K[(X_i)]), } \]

now \(\operatorname{Gr}(f)=\operatorname{Gr}(f_1)\), \(f_1\) being the augmentation homomorphism of \(K[X_i]_{i\geq1}\); since the latter annihilates \(\varphi\), it follows from the analogous diagram written for \(f_1\) that \(H(\operatorname{Gr}(f))\) annihilates the image of \(\varphi\) in \(H\circ\operatorname{Gr}(K[X_i]_{i\geq1})\), whence the result.

By passage to the quotient, we therefore associate to every morphism of functors: \(\widehat G\longrightarrow H\circ\operatorname{Gr}\), a morphism of functors: \(1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+\longrightarrow H\circ\operatorname{Gr}\); by composition with \(j\), and using the isomorphism \(\varepsilon\), we obtain a morphism: \(1+\widehat{\ }^+\longrightarrow H\), which defines the desired homomorphism \(\rho'\). It is immediate to check that \(\rho\) and \(\rho'\) are inverse to each other.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.13.

Let \(H\) be a functor on \(\mathcal C_g\), with values in the category of separated complete filtered abelian groups, and commuting with invariants under a finite group. Then the map from \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}}(1+\widehat{\ }^+,H)\) to \(H(K[X])\) which, to a morphism of functors, associates its value on the element \(1+X\) of \(1+\widehat{K[X]}^+\) is injective; its image is the subgroup of \(H(K[X])\) formed of the elements \(\theta\) such that (notations of 1.4, 1.5):

i) \(H(f_1)(\theta)=0\);

ii) \(\forall n\geq1\), \(H(f_n)(u_H^n(\theta))=u_H^n(\theta)\pmod {F^nH(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n])}\).
Immediate from 1.6 and 1.12.
We have an analogous statement on replacing \(\widehat G\) by \((\widehat G)^k\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.14.

If \(H\) is a functor on \(\mathcal C_g\), with values in the category of abelian groups, commuting with invariants under a finite group, and satisfying the condition:

\[\forall B\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C_g),\quad H(B)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\varprojlim_n H(B/B^{\geq n}) \quad\left(\text{oĂą }B^{\geq n}=\prod_{i\geq n}B^i\right),\]

then condition ii) of 1.13 is always satisfied if we take as filtration on \(H\) the filtration by the kernels of the \(H(B)\longrightarrow H(B/B^{\geq n})\) (which is separated and complete). Indeed, let \(p_n\) be the canonical homomorphism

\[K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\longrightarrow K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]/K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]^{\geq n}\ ;\]

we have: \(p_n\circ f_n=p_n\), whence: \(H(p_n)[H(f_n)\circ u_H^n(\theta)-u_H^n(\theta)]=0\), that is to say:

\[H(f_n)\circ u_H^n(\theta)-u_H^n(\theta)\in F^nH(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]).\]

To give a morphism of functors from \(1+\widehat{\ }^+\) to \(H\), it therefore suffices to give an element of \(H(K[X])\) satisfying only condition i) of 1.13.

Let \(H:\mathcal C\longrightarrow\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}\) (resp. \(\mathcal C_0\longrightarrow\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}\), \(\mathcal C_g\longrightarrow\underline{\mathrm{Ab.fil}}\)) be a functor satisfying the conditions of 1.3 (resp. 1.10, 1.13), and let \(\Phi:(\widehat G)^k\longrightarrow H\) (resp. \((\widehat G\circ i)^k\longrightarrow H\), \((1+\widehat{\ }^+)^k\longrightarrow H\)) be a morphism of functors. We have seen (1.7) that \(\Phi\) is determined by its value on the element \((\varphi^{(j)})_{j=1,\ldots,k}\) of \((\widehat G(K[X_i^{(j)}]_{i\geq1,\,j=1,\ldots,k}))^k\) (resp. ...). Suppose that \(H\) is a functor such that \(\widehat G\), \(\widehat G_g\), \(\widehat G_a\), \(1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+\), etc...; the element \(\Phi((\varphi^{(j)})_j)\) is a formal power series with coefficients in \(K[X_i^{(j)}]_{i\geq1,\,j=1,\ldots,k}\); the \(n\)-th coefficient of this series will be called the \(n\)-th universal polynomial of \(\Phi\); the knowledge of these polynomials is therefore equivalent to the knowledge of \(\Phi\).

According to the proof of 1.6, these polynomials are themselves determined by the element \(\Phi(1+X^{(1)}t,\ldots,1+X^{(k)}t)\), the computation being carried out following the method of the proof: we compute the elements
\[\Phi(1+X_1^{(1)}t+\cdots+X_n^{(1)}t^n,\ldots, 1+X_1^{(k)}t+\cdots+X_n^{(k)}t^n),\]

by decomposing \(1+X_1^{(i)}t+\cdots+X_n^{(i)}t^n\) into \(\prod_{j=1}^n(1+T_j^{(i)}t)\) and using the additivity of \(\Phi\); the polynomials thus obtained are symmetric with respect to the \(T_j^{(i)}\) (\(i\) fixed and \(j\) varying) and can be expressed in terms of the \(X_j^{(i)}\); by glueing by means of the filtration of \(H\), we thus determine the universal polynomials of \(\Phi\). If \((\psi^{(1)},\ldots,\psi^{(k)})\in(\widehat G(A))^k\), the computation of \(\Phi(\psi^{(1)},\ldots,\psi^{(k)})\) is done by substituting the coefficients \(a_j^{(i)}\) of the \(\psi^{(i)}\) for the \(X_j^{(i)}\) in the universal polynomials.

Suppose that there exists an integer \(m\) such that the coefficient of \(t^n\) in \(\theta=\Phi(1+X^{(1)}t,\ldots,1+X^{(k)}t)\) is a polynomial whose isobaric components have a weight varying between \(n\) and \(mn\); then the \(k\)-th universal polynomial of \(\Phi\) has isobaric components whose weight varies between \(k\) and \(km\): this follows immediately from the method of computation explained above, and from the theorem on symmetric functions.

Suppose in particular that \(H=\widehat G\); then the \(n\)-th universal polynomial of \(\Phi\) has no components of weight \(<n\). Let us now consider the canonical morphism: \(H\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+\); by composition with \(\Phi\), we obtain a morphism: \((\widehat G)^k\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+\), which by the proof of 1.12 factors through \((1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+)^k\) and defines a morphism \(\bar\Phi:(1+\widehat{\ }^+)^k\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\ }^+\); we see at once that the \(n\)-th universal polynomial of \(\bar\Phi\) is the same as that of \((\widehat G)^k\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}^+\), that is to say the isobaric component of weight \(n\) of the \(n\)-th universal polynomial of \(\Phi\).

The same remarks apply to the case \(H=\widehat G_a\): one can define a morphism of functors \(H\longrightarrow\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}_a^+\) (with \(\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}}_a^+=\widehat{\ }_a^+\circ\operatorname{Gr}\), \(\widehat{\ }_a^+\) being the functor defined on \(\mathcal C_g\) by \(\widehat B_a^+=\prod_{i\geq1}B^i\)) in a manner analogous to 1.11; one then copies the reasoning and conclusions of the preceding paragraph.

Let us finish with an example. We suppose that \(K=\mathbb Q\). We define an endomorphism of the functor \(1+\widehat{\ }^+\), denoted \(\operatorname{Todd}\), and called the Todd character, by setting:
\[\operatorname{Todd}(1+X)=\frac{X}{1-e^{-X}}.\]
Conditions i) and ii) of Theorem 1.13 are checked immediately, which shows that we have indeed defined in this way an endomorphism of \(1+\widehat{\ }^+\).
2. Definition of \(\lambda\)-rings; examples

In this paragraph, the base ring of paragraph 1 will be \(\mathbb Z\) (except in 2.9); we place ourselves on the category \(\mathcal C_0\) (1.8) and we denote by \(\widehat G\) the restriction of \(\widehat G\) to \(\mathcal C_0\) (1.10).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1.

We call a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring a ring \(K\), commutative with unit element, endowed with a homomorphism of abelian groups:

\[\lambda_t:K\longrightarrow1+K[[t]]^+,\]

such that for every \(x\in K\), \(\lambda_t(x)=1+xt+\cdots\).

We shall set \(\lambda_t(x)=\sum_{i\geq0}\lambda^i(x)t^i\); the datum of a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure on a ring \(K\) is therefore equivalent to the datum of a family of maps \(\lambda^i\), \(i\in\mathbb N\), from \(K\) to \(K\), such that:
i) \(\forall x\in K\), \(\lambda^0(x)=1\),
ii) \(\forall x\in K\), \(\lambda^1(x)=x\),
iii) \(\forall x,y\in K\), \(\forall n\in\mathbb N\), \(\lambda^n(x+y)=\sum_{p+q=n}\lambda^p(x)\lambda^q(y)\).

Let \(K,K'\) be two pre-\(\lambda\)-rings, and \(f:K\longrightarrow K'\) a ring homomorphism; we shall say that \(f\) is a homomorphism of pre-\(\lambda\)-rings, or again a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, if the diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{ K\ar[r]^{\lambda_t}\ar[d]_f&1+K[[t]]^+\ar[d]^{\widehat G(f)}\\ K'\ar[r]_{\lambda_t}&1+K'[[t]]^+ } \]
is commutative.
This is again equivalent to the following relations:
\[\forall x\in K,\ \forall i\in\mathbb N,\qquad \lambda^i(f(x))=f(\lambda^i(x)).\]
Examples 2.2.

a) One can give all the pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structures on \(\mathbb Z\). For this it suffices to give \(\lambda_t(1)\), which will thus be a formal power series with integer coefficients, subject to the sole condition of beginning with \(1+t\).

b) Let \((T,A)\) be a commutatively ringed topos, and let \(K\) be the "Grothendieck group" of \(A\)-Modules locally isomorphic to a direct factor of a free \(A\)-Module of finite type; we know that \(K\) can be endowed with a ring structure by means of the tensor product (Exp. IV), and we shall endow it with a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure in the following way: for every \(A\)-Module \(E\) which is locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type, we denote by \(\lambda^i(E)\) the class in \(K\) of the \(i\)-th exterior power of \(E\) (which is also locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type); this defines a function on the category of \(A\)-Modules which are locally direct factors of a free Module of finite type, with values in the abelian group \(1+K[[t]]^+\); we must then show that this function is additive, which will imply that it factors through the \(K\), whence the desired structure.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.1.

Let \(0\longrightarrow E'\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{u}}E\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{v}}E''\longrightarrow0\) be an exact sequence of \(A\)-Modules which is locally split (for example such that \(E''\) is locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type). There exist on \(\Lambda^n(E)\) a finite filtration, and isomorphisms:

\[\Lambda^{n-p}(E')\otimes_A\Lambda^p(E'')\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Gr}^p\Lambda^n(E).\]
We take for \(F^p\Lambda^n(E)\) the image of the canonical morphism:
\[\Lambda^{n-p}(E')\otimes_A\Lambda^p(E)\longrightarrow\Lambda^n(E)\quad\text{pour }0\leq p\leq n\ ;\]
if \(p>n\), \(F^p\Lambda^n(E)=\Lambda^n(E)\), and if \(p<0\), \(F^p\Lambda^n(E)=0\). This indeed defines a finite filtration on \(\Lambda^n(E)\).
We define a homomorphism:
\[f_p:\Lambda^{n-p}(E')\otimes_A\Lambda^p(E'')\longrightarrow F^p\Lambda^n(E)/F^{p-1}\Lambda^n(E)\]
by setting
\[f_p(x'_1\wedge\cdots\wedge x'_{n-p}\otimes x''_1\wedge\cdots\wedge x''_p) =\operatorname{cl}_{\operatorname{Gr}^p}(u(x'_1)\wedge\cdots\wedge u(x'_{n-p})\wedge x_1\wedge\cdots\wedge x_p)\]

which visibly does not depend on the choice of the \(x_i\) lifting the given sections \(x''_i\) of \(E''\). If the exact sequence is locally split, \(f_p\) is an isomorphism, since locally \(\Lambda^n(E)\simeq\bigoplus_{p+q=n}\Lambda^p(E')\otimes_A\Lambda^q(E'')\).

In the case where \(E',E,E''\) are locally direct factors of free Modules of finite type, the \(\operatorname{Gr}^p\Lambda^n(E)\) are locally direct factors of free Modules of finite type, hence so are the \(F^p\Lambda^n(E)\); we can therefore write in \(K\):
\[\lambda^n(E)=\sum_{p=0}^n\operatorname{cl}(\operatorname{Gr}^p\Lambda^n(E)) =\sum_{p=0}^n\lambda^{n-p}(E')\lambda^p(E''),\]
whence the additivity.

Let \((T,A)\), \((T',A')\) be two commutatively ringed topoi, and \(f:(T,A)\longrightarrow(T',A')\) a morphism of ringed topoi. We know (Exp. IV, 2.7) that \(f\) defines a ring morphism on the corresponding \(K\), say \(f^*:K'_{\mathrm{naĂŻf}}(T',A')\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naĂŻf}}(T,A)\). Since the inverse image commutes with exterior powers, the corresponding morphism \(f^*\) on the \(K\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism for the structure which has just been defined.
Let us point out two particular cases:

- Let \(X\) be a scheme; \(K'_{\mathrm{naĂŻf}}(X)\) is thus endowed with a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure; it is this case which will be used in the rest of the seminar, and it can be regarded as a "model" for the general theory of
\(\lambda\)-rings.

- Let \(A\) be a ring, \(X=\operatorname{Spec}A\); on the other hand let \(G\) be a group, acting trivially on \(A\); if we take for \((T,A)\) the topos of sheaves of sets on \(X\), on which \(G\) acts, the ring \(K\) thus obtained is the ring of representations of \(G\) in projective \(A\)-modules of finite type.

Let \(K\) be a commutative ring with unit element; we propose to endow the abelian group \(\widehat G(K)=1+K[[t]]^+\) with a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure, functorial with respect to \(K\). To this end, we shall apply the results of (1.10) to the functor \(\widehat G\).

We first define the multiplication by giving a \(\mathbb Z\)-bilinear morphism of functors: \(\widehat G\times\widehat G\longrightarrow\widehat G\), by the element \(1+XYt\) of \(\widehat G(\mathbb Z[X,Y])\); indeed this element satisfies 1.7.i), and to see that it satisfies 1.7.ii), we must show that in \(\prod_{i=1}^n(1+T_iYt)\), expressed by means of the elementary symmetric functions \(X_j\) of the \(T_i\), \(X_n\) does not appear in the coefficients of \(t^k\) for \(k<n\), which is obvious for reasons of degree.
The law thus defined will be denoted by the sign \(\circ\); we therefore have by definition:
\[(2.3.1)\qquad(1+Xt)\circ(1+Yt)=1+XYt.\]
It is distributive by construction, and associative and commutative by the diagrams:

\[ \xymatrix{ \widehat G\times\widehat G\times\widehat G\ar[r]\ar[d]&\widehat G\times\widehat G\ar[d]\\ \widehat G\times\widehat G\ar[r]&\widehat G } \qquad \xymatrix{ \widehat G\times\widehat G\ar[r]\ar[d]&\widehat G\times\widehat G\ar[d]\\ \widehat G\ar[r]_{\operatorname{Id}}&\widehat G, }\]

where the meaning of the arrows is obvious, and whose commutativity it suffices to check for the elements \(1+Xt,1+Yt,1+Zt\) of \(\widehat G(\mathbb Z[X,Y,Z])\) (resp. \(1+Xt,1+Xt\) of \(\widehat G(\mathbb Z[X,Y])\)) by 1.10, a check which is immediate.

By 1.8, the product of two series under this law is computed by substituting their coefficients into the universal polynomials of the law. The \(n\)-th universal polynomial will be denoted \(P_n\); it is a polynomial of \(\mathbb Z[X_i,Y_j]_{1\leq i,j\leq n}\), isobaric of weight \(n\) with respect to each of the two groups of variables (see 1.15), and symmetric with respect to these two groups. Let us give as an example the value of \(P_2\):
\[P_2(X_1,X_2;Y_1,Y_2)=X_2Y_1^2+Y_2X_1^2-2X_2Y_2.\]
We also have the following relation: let \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C_0)\), \(\psi\in\widehat G(K)\), \(a\in K\); then
\[(2.3.2)\qquad\psi(t)\circ(1+at)=\psi(at).\]
As usual, it reduces by the standard functorial argument to the case \(K=\mathbb Z[X,Y]\), \(\psi=1+Xt\), \(a=Y\), where it is none other than 2.3.1.

Applying 2.3.2 to \(\widehat G(K)=1+K[[t]]^+\), and to \(a=1\), we see that the element \(1+t\) is a unit for this multiplication. To avoid confusion, we shall sometimes denote it \(1\), or again \(1_\circ\).

The functor \(\widehat G\) can now be regarded as a functor from \(\mathcal C_0\) to the category of commutative rings with unit element, and consequently be composed with itself; to avoid confusion, we shall choose different indeterminates for each of the factors of the product of \(\widehat G\) with itself, and we shall indicate the indeterminate as a superscript when necessary: \(\widehat G^t\), \(\widehat G^u\), etc...

For \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C_0)\), we therefore have \(\widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t(A)=1+\{1+A[[t]]^+\}[[u]]^+\). To define the operations \(\lambda^i\) on \(1+K[[t]]^+\), we shall therefore give ourselves a morphism of functors: \(\widehat G\longrightarrow\widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t\). For this, we must first define a filtration of the functor \(\widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t\) which allows the glueing of 1.6.

We first remark that \(F^k\widehat G\) is an ideal for the law \(\circ\); for this, we are reduced by functoriality to the case \(A=\mathbb Z[X_i,Y_i]_{i\geq1}\), and to the product \((1+\sum_{i\geq k}X_it^i)\circ(1+Yt)\), and this follows from 2.3.2. We then take for \(F^n(\widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t)\) the set of series of the form \(1+\psi_1u+\cdots+\psi_iu^i+\cdots\), \(\psi_i\in F^{[n/i]}\widehat G\), where \([n/i]\) denotes the integer part of \(n/i\).
The element defining the morphism \(\lambda_u\) is \(1+\{1+Xt\}u\); the reader will check as an exercise that the conditions of 1.6 are indeed fulfilled (in fact, the filtration is chosen ad hoc).

Here again, the operations \(\lambda^i\) are computed by substitution from \(\lambda_u(1+\cdots+X_nt^n+\cdots)\), and are therefore determined by universal polynomials. We shall denote by \(Q_{i,j}(X_1,\ldots,X_{ij})\) the coefficient of \(t^j\) in \(\lambda^i(1+\cdots+X_nt^n+\cdots)\); it is an isobaric polynomial of weight \(ij\) with respect to the variables \(X_1,\ldots,X_{ij}\) (a check analogous to that of 1.15). It is computed by symmetric functions from the defining element:
\[(2.3.3)\qquad\lambda_u(1+Xt)=1+\{1+Xt\}u.\]
Let us give as an example the value of \(Q_{2,2}\):
\[Q_{2,2}(X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4)=X_1X_3-X_4.\]

Let us finally indicate the origin of formulas (2.3.1) and (2.3.3). We consider example 2.2.b), where the operations \(\lambda^i\) are defined by the exterior powers of \(A\)-Modules. We wish \(\lambda_t:K\longrightarrow\widehat G(K)\) to be a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism; we must therefore define on \(\widehat G\) laws such that for all \(A\)-Modules \(E,F\), we have:
\[\lambda_t(EF)=\lambda_t(E)\circ\lambda_t(F)\ ;\qquad \lambda_u(\lambda_t(E))=\widehat G^u(\lambda_t)(\lambda_u(E))\ ;\]
now relations (2.3.1) and (2.3.3) are precisely the preceding relations applied to the case where \(E\) and \(F\) are invertible modules.
We shall see in Exp. VI to what extent the desired property is satisfied.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.4.

A pre-\(\lambda\)-ring \(K\) will be called a \(\lambda\)-ring if the homomorphism of abelian groups \(\lambda_t:K\longrightarrow1+K[[t]]^+\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism for the pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure on the second member defined in 2.3.
This therefore means that we have the relations: \(\forall x,y\in K\),
\[(2.4.1)\qquad \lambda_t(1)=1+t\ ;\quad\lambda_t(xy)=\lambda_t(x)\circ\lambda_t(y)\ ;\quad \lambda_u(\lambda_t(x))=\widehat G(\lambda_t)(\lambda_u(x)).\]
In terms of the maps \(\lambda^i\), the last two relations are equivalent to: \(x,y\in K\), \(i,j\in\mathbb N\),

\[(2.4.2)\qquad \begin{aligned} \lambda^i(xy)&=P_i(\lambda^1(x),\ldots,\lambda^i(x);\lambda^1(y),\ldots,\lambda^i(y)),\\ \lambda^j(\lambda^i(x))&=Q_{i,j}(\lambda^1(x),\ldots,\lambda^{ij}(x)). \end{aligned} \]

It will be noted that we depart here from the usual terminology (cf. [1]), where what we call a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring was called a \(\lambda\)-ring, and our \(\lambda\)-rings were called "special" \(\lambda\)-rings; the reason is that only the "special" \(\lambda\)-rings are usable in practice.
Examples 2.5.

a) We have determined all the pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structures on \(\mathbb Z\); it follows from 2.4.1 that only the one defined by \(\lambda_t(1)=1+t\) can possibly be a \(\lambda\)-ring structure. We then have \(\lambda_t(n)=(1+t)^n\), whence:
\[ \forall n,n'\in\mathbb Z,\quad \lambda_t(n)\circ\lambda_t(n')=(1+t)^n\circ(1+t)^{n'}=(1+t)^{nn'}=\lambda_t(nn')\ ; \]
\[ \lambda_u((1+t)^n)=(\lambda_u(1+t))^n=(1+\{1+t\}u)^n =\widehat G(\lambda_t)((1+u)^n)=\widehat G(\lambda_t)(\lambda_u(n)). \]
Consequently it is indeed a \(\lambda\)-ring structure. If we denote by \(\binom Xk\) the polynomial with rational coefficients \(\frac1{k!}X(X-1)\cdots(X-k+1)\), we therefore have:
\[(2.5.1)\qquad\lambda^i(n)=\binom ni.\]

b) For every ring \(K\), the pre-\(\lambda\)-ring \(1+K[[t]]^+\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring. First, \(\lambda_u(1)=\lambda_u(1+t)=1+\{1+t\}u=1+1\,u\). We check the other relations by the commutativity of the diagrams:

\[ \xymatrix{ \widehat G^t\times\widehat G^t\ar[r]^{\lambda_u\times\lambda_u}\ar[d]_{\circ}& (\widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t)\times(\widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t)\ar[d]^{\circ}\\ \widehat G^t\ar[r]_{\lambda_u}&\widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t } \qquad \xymatrix{ \widehat G^t\ar[r]^{\lambda_v}\ar[d]_{\lambda_u}&\widehat G^v\circ\widehat G^t\ar[d]^{\widehat G^v(\lambda_u)}\\ \widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t\ar[r]_{\lambda_v}& \widehat G^v\circ\widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t, }\]
which is checked, by 1.10, on the elements \(1+Xt,1+Yt\). For the first,
\[ \begin{aligned} \lambda_u(1+Xt)\circ\lambda_u(1+Yt) &=(1+\{1+Xt\}u)\circ(1+\{1+Yt\}u)\\ &=1+\{(1+Xt)\circ(1+Yt)\}u\\ &=1+\{1+XYt\}u\\ &=\lambda_u((1+Xt)\circ(1+Yt)). \end{aligned} \]
For the second:
\[\lambda_v(\lambda_u(1+Xt)) =1+\{1+\{1+Xt\}u\}v =\widehat G^v(\lambda_u)(\lambda_v(1+Xt)).\]
c) The geometric examples of 2.2 will be treated later.
We can generalize example 2.5.a) in the following way:
Let \(K\) be a commutative ring with unit element, such that:
i) \(K\) is torsion-free,
ii) \(\forall x\in K\), \(\forall n\in\mathbb N\), we have \(\binom xn\in K\).
We then set, for every \(x\in K\) and every \(n\in\mathbb N\), \(\lambda^n(x)=\binom xn\), that is:
\[(2.6.1)\qquad \lambda_t(x)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\binom xn t^n=(1+t)^x=\exp(x\operatorname{Log}(1+t)),\]
where the last identity is considered in \(K\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q[[t]]\).
By the multiplicativity of the exponential, this defines a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure on \(K\).

To see that it is a \(\lambda\)-ring structure, we must prove relations 2.4.2, and it suffices to prove them in \(K\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\), by 2.6.i). We are then reduced by functoriality to the case \(K=\mathbb Q[X,Y]\), and to the elements \(X,Y\). Taking into account the definition of the \(\lambda^i\), the two members in the equalities 2.4.2 are polynomials in \(X,Y\) with rational coefficients, and it therefore suffices, in order to show their equality, to show that they take the same value for all integer values of \(X,Y\). But the relations obtained by substituting integer values for \(X,Y\) are those expressing that the pre-\(\lambda\)-ring \(\mathbb Z\) (2.5.a)) is a \(\lambda\)-ring; they are therefore satisfied.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.7.

We call a binomial ring a ring satisfying conditions 2.6.i) and ii), endowed with the \(\lambda\)-ring structure defined by 2.6.1.
Example 2.8.
Let \(X\) be a site, \(K\) a binomial ring, and \(\widehat K\) the sheaf associated to the constant presheaf with value \(K\) on \(X\); then \(H^0(X,\widehat K)\) is a binomial ring.
Indeed, conditions 2.6 are preserved under passage to the inductive limit.
In particular, \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) is a binomial ring.

Let \(K\) be a binomial ring, \(A\) a \(K\)-algebra. For every series \(\varphi\in\widehat G(A)\), and every \(a\in K\), one can define a series \(\varphi^a\) in the usual way: we write \(\varphi=1+\psi\), and we set:
\[(2.9.1)\qquad \varphi^a=(1+\psi)^a=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\binom an\psi^n \quad(=\exp(a\operatorname{Log}\varphi)),\]

the last equality only being writable modulo torsion. These formulas are obtained by substitution from the analogous formulas for \(\varphi=1+t\); the following relations, classical properties of the exponential, therefore still hold for \(a,b\in K\), \(\varphi,\psi\in\widehat G(A)\):
\[(2.9.2)\qquad \varphi^a\varphi^b=\varphi^{a+b}\ ;\quad \varphi^{ab}=(\varphi^a)^b\ ;\quad \varphi^a\psi^a=(\varphi\psi)^a.\]

We still suppose that \(a\in K\). Multiplication for the law \(\circ\) by \(\lambda_t(a)\) defines a morphism of functors: \(\widehat G_K\longrightarrow\widehat G_K\), characterized by its value for the element \(1+Xt\) of \(\widehat G(K[X])\). We therefore have:
\[\lambda_t(a)\circ(1+Xt)=\lambda_{Xt}(a)\quad\text{d'aprĂ¨s (2.3.2), donc :}\]
\[(2.9.3)\qquad \lambda_t(a)\circ(1+Xt)=(1+Xt)^a=\exp(a\operatorname{Log}(1+Xt)) =\sum_{n=0}^\infty\binom anX^nt^n.\]
From this we deduce:
\[(2.9.4)\qquad \lambda_t(a)\circ\varphi=\varphi^a\quad(=\exp(a\operatorname{Log}\varphi)).\]
Indeed, the two members are additive functors which are equal for \(1+Xt\).
From (2.9.3) and 1.15, it follows that the \(n\)-th universal polynomial defining multiplication by \(\lambda_t(a)\) is isobaric of weight \(n\); it will be denoted \(R_{n,a}\).
Let us finally note that if \(A\) is a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra, i.e. a \(\lambda\)-ring endowed with a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism: \(K\longrightarrow A\), and if \(b\in A\), we have:
\[(2.9.5)\qquad\lambda_t(ab)=(\lambda_t(b))^a.\]
Indeed, this follows from \(\lambda_t(ab)=\lambda_t(a)\circ\lambda_t(b)\), and from (2.9.4).
3. The operations \(\gamma\)
We again place ourselves on the category \(\mathcal C\).

Let \(K\) be a \(\lambda\)-ring. Starting from the operations \(\lambda^i\) of \(K\) we shall define new operations, denoted \(\gamma^i\). They are intended to introduce a notion of Chern classes under conditions which will be indicated later. For the moment, let us give a "justification" of the definitions which follow: if \(E\) is a vector bundle of rank \(d\), the \(d\)-th Chern class of \(E\) should be (whatever the theory of Chern classes envisaged) the "alternating sum of the exterior powers

of \(E\)"; to define the \(n\)-th Chern class, we shall proceed in an analogous way, bringing \(E\) down to rank \(n\) by considering \((\operatorname{cl}(E)-d)+n\), and we shall take the alternating sum of the exterior powers of the element thus obtained; the following proposition will be "interpreted" by taking \(x=\operatorname{cl}(E)-d\) in it:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2.

Let \(K\) be a \(\lambda\)-ring.

i) \(\forall x\in K\), \(\forall n\in\mathbb N\),

\[\lambda^n(x+n-1)=(-1)^n\sum_{i=0}^n(-1)^i\lambda^i(x+n).\]

ii) If we set \(\gamma^n(x)=\lambda^n(x+n-1)\), and \(\gamma_t(x)=\sum_{n\geq0}\gamma^n(x)t^n\), then:

\[\gamma_t(x)=\lambda_{t/(1-t)}(x),\quad\text{donc}\quad \lambda_t(x)=\gamma_{t/(1+t)}(x).\]
We therefore see that the knowledge of the operations \(\gamma\) is equivalent to that of the operations \(\lambda\).
Proof.
i) We have
\[\lambda^n(x+n-1)=\sum_{i=0}^n\lambda^i(x+n)\lambda^{n-i}(-1).\]
From \(\lambda_t(1)=1+t\), we obtain: \(\lambda_t(-1)=1/(1+t)\), whence: \(\lambda^k(-1)=(-1)^k\), which gives the desired relation.
ii) \(\lambda_{t/(1-t)}(x)=\sum_{n\geq0}\lambda^n(x)t^n(1-t)^{-n}\).
We expand \(t^n(1-t)^{-n}=t^n\sum_{k\geq0}\binom{-n}k(-1)^kt^k=\sum_{p\geq n}\binom{p-1}{p-n}t^p\).
\[\lambda_{t/(1-t)}(x) =\sum_{n\geq0}\left\{\lambda^n(x)\sum_{p\geq n}\binom{p-1}{p-n}t^p\right\}\ ;\]
the coefficient of \(t^p\) is therefore:
\[\sum_{n=0}^p\lambda^n(x)\binom{p-1}{p-n} =\sum_{n=0}^p\lambda^n(x)\lambda^{p-n}(p-1)=\lambda^p(x+p-1).\]

Let \(K,K'\) be two \(\lambda\)-rings, and \(f:K\longrightarrow K'\) a ring homomorphism. By definition 3.2.ii), \(f\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism if and only if for every \(i\in\mathbb N\) and every \(x\in K\), \(f(\gamma^i(x))=\gamma^i(f(x))\), or again \(\gamma_t(f(x))=\widehat G_g(f)\circ\gamma_t(x)\).
From relation 3.2.ii), we deduce the relations:
\[(3.4.1)\qquad \gamma^0(x)=1,\quad\gamma^1(x)=x,\quad \gamma^n(x+y)=\sum_{p+q=n}\gamma^p(x)\gamma^q(y).\]

They show that the homomorphism \(\gamma_t\) defines on \(K\) a new pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure; we shall see that it is not in general a \(\lambda\)-ring structure. If we denote by \(\rho_t\) the substitution on the ring of formal power series in \(t\) with coefficients in \(K\) defined by \(\rho_t(t)=t/(1-t)\), we have:
\[\gamma_t=\rho_t\circ\lambda_t.\]

The automorphism \(\rho_t\) is functorial with respect to \(K\), and defines an automorphism of the functor \(\widehat G\) regarded as a functor in abelian groups; with the help of this automorphism, one can, by transport of structure, define on \(\widehat G\) a new structure of functor in \(\lambda\)-rings, which will be such that for every \(\lambda\)-ring \(K\), the homomorphism \(\gamma_t:K\longrightarrow\widehat G(K)\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism; we shall make explicit the multiplication and the operations \(\lambda^i\) of this second structure.
The multiplication is defined by the homomorphism of functors:

\[\widehat G\times\widehat G \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{(\rho^{-1},\rho^{-1})}}\widehat G\times\widehat G \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\circ}}\widehat G \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\rho}}\widehat G,\]
where the sign \(\circ\) denotes the multiplication defined in 2.3. This new multiplication will be denoted \(\nabla\). It is as usual defined by its value on \((1+Xt,1+Yt)\); we therefore have:

\[(3.5.1)\qquad \begin{aligned} (1+Xt)\nabla(1+Yt) &=\rho\bigl(\rho^{-1}(1+Xt)\circ\rho^{-1}(1+Yt)\bigr)\\ &=\rho\left(\frac{1+(X+1)t}{1+t}\circ\frac{1+(Y+1)t}{1+t}\right)\\ &=\rho\left(\frac{(1+(X+1)(Y+1)t)(1+t)}{(1+(X+1)t)(1+(Y+1)t)}\right)\\ &=\frac{1+(1+X+Y+XY)t}{(1+Xt)(1+Yt)}. \end{aligned}\]
If \(K\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring, we therefore have for all \(x,y\in K\):
\[(3.5.2)\qquad\gamma_t(xy)=\gamma_t(x)\nabla\gamma_t(y).\]

The \(n\)-th universal polynomial of the law \(\nabla\) will be denoted \(P'_n\); it is a polynomial of \(\mathbb Z[X_i,Y_j]_{1\leq i,j\leq n}\), whose isobaric components have weight varying from \(n\) to \(2n\) with respect to the set of variables \(X_i,Y_j\), (1.15) and symmetric with respect to these two groups of variables. For \(x,y\in K\), we therefore have:
\[(3.5.3)\qquad n\in\mathbb N,\quad \gamma^n(xy)=P'_n(\gamma^1(x),\ldots,\gamma^n(x),\gamma^1(y),\ldots,\gamma^n(y)).\]
Let us finally note that the law \(\nabla\) admits a unit element, which is \(\rho_t(1+t)\), namely \(1/(1-t)\); it will sometimes be denoted \(1_\nabla\).
The "operations \(\lambda\)" are defined by the commutative diagram:
\[(3.6.1)\qquad \xymatrix{ 1+K[[t]]^+\ar[r]^{\lambda_u}\ar[d]_{\rho_t}& 1+\{1+K[[t]]^+\}[[u]]^+\ar[d]^{\widehat G(\rho_t)}\\ 1+K[[t]]^+\ar[r]_{\lambda'_u}& 1+\{1+K[[t]]^+\}[[u]]^+, }\]
i.e. by the composite morphism of functors:

\[\lambda':\widehat G \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\rho_t^{-1}}}\widehat G \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\lambda_u}}\widehat G\circ\widehat G \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\widehat G(\rho_t)}}\widehat G\circ\widehat G,\]
and will be denoted \(\lambda'^i\). They are defined by their value for \(1+Xt\); we have:

\[ \begin{aligned} \lambda'_u(1+Xt) &=\widehat G(\rho_t)(\lambda_u(\rho_t^{-1}(1+Xt)))\\ &=\widehat G(\rho_t)\left(\lambda_u\left(\frac{1+(X+1)t}{1+t}\right)\right)\\ &=\widehat G(\rho_t)\left(\frac{1_\circ+\{1+(X+1)t\}u}{1_\circ+\{1+t\}u}\right)\\ &=\frac{1_\nabla+\{1+Xt\}u}{1_\nabla+1_\nabla u}\\ &=\rho_u^{-1}(1_\nabla+\{1+Xt\}u). \end{aligned} \]
If we denote by \(\gamma'\) the operations \(\gamma\) corresponding to the operations \(\lambda'\), we therefore have:
\[(3.6.2)\qquad\gamma'_u(1+Xt)=1_\nabla+\{1+Xt\}u.\]

We shall denote by \(Q'_{i,j}\) the coefficient of \(t^j\) in \(\gamma'^i(1+\cdots+X_nt^n+\cdots)\); the reader may check that it is a polynomial of \(\mathbb Z[X_1,\ldots,X_{ij}]\), whose isobaric components have weight varying between \(j\) and \(ij\).
Let \(K\) be a \(\lambda\)-ring, \(x\in K\). From diagram (3.6.1), we deduce the relations:
\[(3.6.3)\qquad\forall i\in\mathbb N,\quad \gamma_t(\lambda^i(x))=\lambda'^i(\gamma_t(x))\ ;\quad \gamma_t(\gamma^i(x))=\gamma'^i(\gamma_t(x)).\]
\[(3.6.4)\qquad \gamma^j(\gamma^i(x))=Q'_{i,j}(\gamma^1(x),\ldots,\gamma^{ij}(x)).\]

We shall finally remark that the pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure which has just been defined on \(\widehat G\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring structure, since it is deduced from the one defined in 2.3 by the isomorphism \(\rho\).

To avoid confusion, the functor \(\widehat G\), endowed with the structure of functor in \(\lambda\)-rings defined in Â§2 (resp. in Â§3), will be denoted \(\widehat G_\circ\) (resp. \(\widehat G_\nabla\)). It follows from the preceding considerations that to give a

\(\lambda\)-ring structure on a ring \(A\), it amounts to the same to give a ring homomorphism \(A\longrightarrow\widehat G(A)_\circ\) such that diagram 3.7.1 is commutative, or a ring homomorphism \(A\longrightarrow\widehat G(A)_\nabla\) such that diagram 3.7.2 is commutative; the first defines the operations \(\lambda\), the second the operations \(\gamma\).

\[(3.7.1)\qquad \xymatrix{ A\ar[r]^{\lambda_u}\ar[d]_{\lambda_t}&\widehat G^u(A)_\circ\ar[d]^{\widehat G^u(\lambda_t)}\\ \widehat G^t(A)_\circ\ar[r]_{\lambda_u}&\widehat G^u(\widehat G^t(A)_\circ)_\circ }\]

\[(3.7.2)\qquad \xymatrix{ A\ar[r]^{\gamma_u}\ar[d]_{\gamma_t}&\widehat G^u(A)_\nabla\ar[d]^{\widehat G^u(\gamma_t)}\\ \widehat G^t(A)_\nabla\ar[r]_{\gamma'_u}&\widehat G^u(\widehat G^t(A)_\nabla)_\nabla. }\]
Let \(K\) be a binomial ring (2.7); from (2.6.1) we deduce \(\forall x\in K\) the relations:
\[(3.8.1)\qquad\gamma_t(x)=(1/(1-t))^x=\exp(-x\operatorname{Log}(1-t)).\]
Let \(a\) be an element of \(K\); multiplication by \(\gamma_t(a)\) for the law \(\nabla\) is characterized by its value for \(1+Xt\), namely:
\[ \gamma_t(a)\nabla(1+Xt)=\rho_t\left(\lambda_t(a)\circ_\circ\frac{1+(X+1)t}{1+t}\right) =\rho_t\left(\left(\frac{1+(X+1)t}{1+t}\right)^a\right). \]
\[(3.8.2)\qquad \gamma_t(a)\nabla(1+Xt)=(1+Xt)^a=\exp(a\operatorname{Log}(1+Xt)) =\sum_{n=0}^\infty\binom anX^nt^n.\]
In particular, we see that the \(n\)-th universal polynomial defining multiplication by \(\gamma_t(a)\) for the law \(\nabla\) is the polynomial \(R_{n,a}\) (2.9).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.9.

Let \(K\) be a \(\lambda\)-ring; we call an augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra the datum of a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra \(A\) and of a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism of \(K\)-algebras \(A\longrightarrow K\); a homomorphism of augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebras is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism compatible with the augmentations.

Let \((X,A)\) be a commutatively locally ringed topos, and \(K'(X)\) the Grothendieck group of the category of sheaves of \(A\)-modules locally free of finite type on \(X\). We have seen that \(K'(X)\) can be endowed with a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure (2.2.b, since every direct factor of a free Module of finite type is then locally free of finite type) and that \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) is canonically endowed with a binomial ring structure. We define a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism from \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) to \(K'(X)\) in the following way: let \(a\) be a section of the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z\) on \(X\); it defines a partition of the final object of \(X\) into a sum of disjoint open objects \(U_n\), the section \(a\) having value \(n\) on the open object \(U_n\); then let \(F\) be the sheaf equal to \(A^n\) on \(U_n\) for \(n\geq0\), and to 0 on \(U_n\) for \(n\leq0\), and let \(F'\) be the sheaf equal to \(A^{-n}\) on \(U_n\) for \(n\leq0\), and to 0 on \(U_n\) for \(n\geq0\); we associate to \(a\) the element \(F-F'\) of \(K'(X)\).

It is immediate to check that in this way we indeed obtain a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism from \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) to \(K'(X)\); we can then define the augmentation from \(K'(X)\) to \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\), by associating to every locally free sheaf of finite type its rank, which is a section of \(\mathbb Z\); the additivity of the rank implies that we thus define a function on \(K'(X)\), with values in \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\). We have therefore endowed \(K'(X)\) with a structure of augmented pre-\(\lambda\)-algebra. It is always this structure that will be in question in what follows.

Let \(A\) be an augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra. We define on \(A\) a ring filtration in the following manner: for every \(n>0\), we denote by \(\operatorname{Fil}^nA\) the ideal generated by the products:
\[(3.10.1)\qquad \gamma^{i_1}(x_1)\cdots\gamma^{i_k}(x_k),\quad \text{avec }i_1+\cdots+i_k\geq n,\quad\text{et }\forall i,\ \varepsilon(x_i)=0\]
(\(\varepsilon:A\longrightarrow K\) being the augmentation).
Suppose that \(A\) is generated as an abelian group by a family of elements \((x_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\); then \(\operatorname{Fil}^nA\) is generated as a \(K\)-module by the products:
\[(3.10.2)\qquad \gamma^{i_1}(x_{\alpha_1}-\varepsilon(x_{\alpha_1}))\cdots \gamma^{i_k}(x_{\alpha_k}-\varepsilon(x_{\alpha_k})),\quad \text{avec }i_1+\cdots+i_k\geq n,\quad\alpha_i\in I.\]

Indeed, if \(x\) is an element of \(A\) with zero augmentation and if we write \(x=\sum_i x_{\alpha_i}\), we have \(\sum_i\varepsilon(x_{\alpha_i})=0\), hence \(x=\sum_i x_{\alpha_i}-\varepsilon(x_{\alpha_i})\); moreover, if \(a\in A\), we can write \(a=(a-\varepsilon(a))+\varepsilon(a)=\gamma^1(a-\varepsilon(a))+\varepsilon(a)\); using relations (3.4.1), we therefore see that the elements of \(\operatorname{Fil}^nA\) are linear combinations with coefficients in \(K\) of the elements (3.10.2); since the latter are in \(\operatorname{Fil}^nA\), we obtain the desired result.
The filtration thus defined is a ring filtration; it is decreasing, and we have:
\[\operatorname{Fil}^0A=A\ ;\qquad\operatorname{Fil}^1A=\ker\varepsilon.\]

Moreover, it is functorial with respect to \(A\): every \(\lambda\)-homomorphism of augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebras is compatible with the filtrations. When we regard an augmented \(\lambda\)-ring as endowed with a filtration, it will be, unless expressly stated otherwise, the filtration above.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.11.

Let \(A\) be a \(\lambda\)-ring, and \(y_i\), \(i=1,\ldots,n\) elements of \(A\) such that \(\lambda^j(y_i)=0\) for \(j\geq2\). We set \(x=\sum_i y_i\); then:

i) for \(k>n\), we have \(\gamma^k(x-n)=0\);

ii) for \(1\leq k\leq n\), \(\gamma^k(x-n)\) is the \(k\)-th symmetric function of the \(y_i-1\).

Let \(j\geq2\). We then have \(\gamma^j(y_i-1)=\lambda^j(y_i+j-2)=\sum_{p+q=j}\lambda^p(y_i)\lambda^q(j-2)\). Since \(\lambda^p(y_i)=0\) for \(p\geq2\), and \(\lambda^q(j-2)=0\) for \(q>j-2\), \(\gamma^j(y_i-1)=0\).
The proposition then follows from the fact that \(\gamma_t(x-n)=\prod_{i=1}^n\gamma_t(y_i-1)\).
4. \(\lambda\)-rings generated by generators and relations
We still place ourselves on \(\mathcal C_0\).

Let \(K\) be a \(\lambda\)-ring. We consider a family of indeterminates \((X_i)_{i\in I}\), and let \(A=K[X_i,\lambda^nX_i]_{i\in I,n\geq2}\); we want to define on \(A\) a \(\lambda\)-ring structure extending that of \(K\).

To give a homomorphism \(\lambda_t:A\longrightarrow\widehat G(A)\), it suffices to give the images of the \(X_i,\lambda^nX_i\), which defines a ring homomorphism: \(A\longrightarrow\widehat G(A)_\circ\), and in order for it to define a \(\lambda\)-ring structure on \(A\), we must check the commutativity of diagram (3.7.1). We therefore set:
\[(4.1.1)\qquad \lambda_t(X_i)=1+X_it+\sum_{n\geq2}\lambda^nX_i\,t^n\ ;\qquad \lambda_t(\lambda^nX_i)=\lambda^n(\lambda_t(X_i)).\]

Diagram (3.7.1) being a diagram of ring homomorphisms, it suffices to check its commutativity for the \(X_i,\lambda^nX_i\) (it is known by hypothesis on the elements of \(K\)), which follows easily from the definitions (4.1.1) and from the fact that \(\lambda_t\) and \(\widehat G^u(\lambda_t)\) are \(\lambda\)-homomorphisms.
The \(\lambda\)-ring \(A\) is characterized by the following universal property:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2.

For every \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra \(B\), and every family \((b_i)_{i\in I}\) of elements of \(B\), there exists one and only one \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-homomorphism \(f\) from \(A\) to \(B\) such that for every \(i\in I\), \(f(X_i)=b_i\).

For \(f\) to be a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, we must have \(f(\lambda^jX_i)=\lambda^j(b_i)\) for all \(i,j\); this defines \(f\) uniquely. The homomorphism thus obtained is indeed a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, since to check that \(\lambda_t\circ f=\widehat G(f)\circ\lambda_t\) it suffices to check it on a system of generators of \(A\) over \(K\), which follows from the definition of \(f\) and relations (2.4.2).

We can carry out the same construction using the \(\gamma\) instead of the \(\lambda\). Let \(A'=K[X_i,\gamma^nX_i]_{i\in I,n\geq2}\); to define the \(\lambda\)-ring structure of \(A'\), we give ourselves a ring homomorphism: \(A'\longrightarrow\widehat G(A')_\nabla\), by:
\[(4.3.1)\qquad \gamma_t(X_i)=1+X_it+\sum_{n\geq2}\gamma^nX_i\,t^n\ ;\qquad \gamma_t(\gamma^nX_i)=\gamma'^n(\gamma_t(X_i)).\]
We then check the commutativity of (3.7.2), which shows that we indeed obtain a \(\lambda\)-ring structure.
The \(\lambda\)-ring \(A'\) also satisfies the universal property 4.2; it is therefore canonically isomorphic (as a \(\lambda\)-ring) to the \(\lambda\)-ring \(A\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.4.

The \(\lambda\)-ring \(A\) will be called the free \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(K\) by the family of generators \((X_i)_{i\in I}\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.5.

Let \(A\) be a \(\lambda\)-ring, \(J\) an ideal of \(A\). We say that \(J\) is a \(\lambda\)-ideal of \(A\) if for every \(x\in J\) and every \(n\geq1\), \(\lambda^n(x)\in J\).
If \(f:A\longrightarrow B\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, the kernel of \(f\) is a \(\lambda\)-ideal.

Let \(J\) be a \(\lambda\)-ideal; the composite homomorphism: \(A\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\lambda_t}}\widehat G(A)\longrightarrow\widehat G(A/J)\) vanishes on \(J\) and therefore factors through \(A/J\), which is thus endowed with a \(\lambda\)-ring structure, the relations (2.4.2) passing to the quotient.

Every intersection of \(\lambda\)-ideals is a \(\lambda\)-ideal. In particular, there exists a smallest \(\lambda\)-ideal containing a given family of elements \((x_i)_{i\in I}\); by relations (2.4.2), it is the (ordinary) ideal generated by the \(\lambda^n(x_i)\), \((n\geq1)\).

Let \(K\) still be a \(\lambda\)-ring, \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) a family of indeterminates, \(A=K[(X_i),(\lambda^nX_i)]\), and let \((P_{i'})_{i'\in I'}\) be a family of elements of \(A\); let \(J\) be the \(\lambda\)-ideal generated by the \(P_{i'}\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.6.

The \(\lambda\)-ring \(A/J\) will be called the \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(K\) by the family of generators \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) subject to the relations \((P_{i'})_{i'\in I'}\).

One can also give relations between the \(X_i,\gamma^nX_i\) and carry out the same construction, remarking that an ideal is a \(\lambda\)-ideal if and only if it is stable under the operations \(\gamma^n\), \(n\geq1\), as follows from the relations 3.2.

Let \(B\) be a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra, \((b_i)_{i\in I}\) a family of elements of \(B\). The \(P_{i'}\) being polynomials with respect to the indeterminates \(X_i,\lambda^nX_i\), one can substitute the \(b_i,\lambda^n(b_i)\) for these indeterminates; if the element obtained is zero, we shall say that the \(b_i\) satisfy the relations \(P_{i'}\). The \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra \(A/J\) is then characterized by the following universal property:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.

For every \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra \(B\) and every family \((b_i)_{i\in I}\) of elements of \(B\) satisfying the relations \((P_{i'})_{i'\in I'}\), there exists one and only one \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-homomorphism \(A/J\longrightarrow B\) such that for every \(i\), we have \(f'(X_i)=b_i\).

Indeed, the kernel of the homomorphism \(f\) defined in 4.2 contains the \(P_{i'}\), hence \(J\) since it is a \(\lambda\)-ideal; \(f\) therefore passes to the quotient and defines \(f'\), which is obviously unique.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.8.

Let \(n\) be an integer \(>0\); in the free \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(K\) by an indeterminate \(X\), the ideal generated by the \(\lambda^kX\), \(k>n\), is a \(\lambda\)-ideal.

Let us set \(A=K[X,\lambda^kX]_{k\geq2}\), and let \(J\) be the ideal generated by the \(\lambda^kX\) for \(k>n\); we set \(B=A/J\). We must show that the composite homomorphism: \(A\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\lambda_t}}\widehat G(A)\longrightarrow\widehat G(B)\) vanishes on \(J\), i.e. that \(\lambda_t(\lambda^kX)\), \(k>n\), has image 1 in \(\widehat G(B)\). Now \(\lambda_t(\lambda^kX)=\lambda^k(\lambda_t(X))\), and \(\widehat G(A)\longrightarrow\widehat G(B)\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism; it therefore suffices to show that for every ring \(C\) and every series \(\varphi\in\widehat G(C)\) whose coefficients vanish in degrees \(>n\), \(\lambda^k(\varphi)=1\) if \(k>n\). But to prove this last property it suffices to show it for \(C=K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\), and \(\varphi=1+X_1t+\cdots+X_nt^n\). Embedding \(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\) in \(K[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\) by means of the symmetric functions, we can write: \(1+X_1t+\cdots+X_nt^n=\prod_{i=1}^n(1+T_it)\), whence
\[\lambda_u(1+X_1t+\cdots+X_nt^n)=\prod_{i=1}^n\lambda_u(1+T_it)=\prod_{i=1}^n(1+\{1+T_it\}u),\]
which is of degree \(n\) in \(u\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9.

The \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(K\) by the generator \(X\) subject to the relations \(\lambda^k(X)=0\) for \(k>n\) is isomorphic to \(K[X,\ldots,\lambda^nX]\).

We would have the same statements on replacing the \(\lambda\) by the \(\gamma\). One can also replace \(X\) by a family of indeterminates \((X_i)_{i\in I}\), subject to the relations \(\lambda^k(X_i)=0\) for \(k>n_i\), the \(n_i\) being integers \(\geq2\); the \(\lambda\)-ring obtained is then \(K[X_i,\lambda^kX_i]_{i\in I,2\leq k\leq n_i}\).

Let \(K\) be a binomial ring, and \(A\) an augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra. We denote by \(B=A[X,\gamma^kX]_{k\geq2}\) the free \(\lambda\)-ring generated by \(X\) over \(A\); we define a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism: \(B\longrightarrow K\) extending the augmentation of \(A\) by sending \(X\) to 0, which endows \(B\) with a structure of augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra. We propose to determine its filtration (3.10) in terms of that of \(A\).

We grade \(B\) by the weight with respect to the \(\gamma^kX\), \(\gamma^kX\) being assigned the weight \(k\); we denote by \(M_k\) the set of monomials of weight \(k\) with respect to the indeterminates \(\gamma^iX\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.11.

Let \(a\in A\); the coefficient of a monomial of weight \(n\) with respect to the \(\gamma^jX\) in \(\gamma^k(aX)=P'_k(\gamma^i(a),\gamma^jX)\) is of filtration at least \(k-n\) (in \(A\)).
The polynomial \(P'_k\) has no isobaric components of weight \(<k\) with respect to the \(\gamma^i(a),\gamma^jX\) (3.5), so the lemma is true if \(a\) has zero augmentation.
If \(\varepsilon\) denotes the augmentation homomorphism: \(A\longrightarrow K\), we can write in the general case:
\[\gamma_t(aX)=\gamma_t((a-\varepsilon(a))X)\,\gamma_t(\varepsilon(a)X).\]

Now \(\gamma_t(\varepsilon(a)X)=(\gamma_t(X))^{\varepsilon(a)}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty R_{n,a}((\gamma^jX))t^n\), the \(R_{n,a}\) being isobaric of weight \(n\) with respect to the \(\gamma^jX\) (3.8); we deduce the lemma from this by taking the product.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.12.

\(\operatorname{Fil}^kB=\operatorname{Fil}^kA+\operatorname{Fil}^{k-1}A\,M_1+\cdots+\operatorname{Fil}^0A\,M_k+B\,M_{k+1}\).
It is clear that the second ideal is contained in the first; it therefore remains simply to show the converse.

Since the filtration defined by the family of ideals of the second member for varying \(k\) is a ring filtration, it suffices to show the converse for an element of the type \(\gamma^k(b)\), with \(\varepsilon(b)=0\), these elements generating the canonical filtration.

Writing \(b=\sum_i a_i m_i\), with \(a_i\in A\), and the \(m_i\) being monomials with respect to the \(\gamma^jX\), and using the additivity of \(\gamma_t\), we see that it suffices to carry out the proof for \(\gamma^k(am_i)\), with \(a\in A\) and \(m_i\in M_i\).
Using Lemma 4.11, and the \(A\)-\(\lambda\)-homomorphism from \(B\) to \(B\) which sends \(X\) to \(m_i\), we see that it suffices to carry out the proof for an element of the type \(\gamma^k(m_i)\).

Finally, the polynomial \(P'_k\) having no isobaric components of weight \(<k\), we are reduced to the case \(\gamma^k(\gamma^jX)\), which itself follows from the fact that the polynomials \(Q'_{j,k}\) have no isobaric components of weight \(<k\) (3.6).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 4.13.

i) The decomposition of \(\operatorname{Fil}^kB\) given in 4.12 is a decomposition into a direct sum of \(A\)-modules. In other words, if \(b\in\operatorname{Fil}^kB\), and if we write \(b=\sum_{j\leq k}a_{i,j}m_{i,j}+b'\), with \(a_{i,j}\in A\), \(m_{i,j}\in M_j\), and \(b'\in B M_{k+1}\), then \(a_{i,j}\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k-j}A\).

ii) Statement 4.12 remains true for every quotient of \(B\) by a \(\lambda\)-ideal contained in the augmentation ideal.

iii) The statement analogous to 4.12, obtained by replacing the indeterminate \(X\) by a family of indeterminates \((X_i)\), is also true.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.14.

\(\operatorname{Gr}^kB\simeq\operatorname{Gr}^kA\oplus\operatorname{Gr}^{k-1}A.M_1\oplus\cdots\oplus\operatorname{Gr}^0A.M_k\).

We define an arrow from right to left by associating to every element \(\bar a.m\), where \(\bar a\) is the class in \(\operatorname{Gr}^{k-i}A\) of an element \(a\) of \(\operatorname{Fil}^{k-i}A\) and \(m\in M_i\), the class in \(\operatorname{Gr}^kB\) of \(am\). We define an arrow in the opposite direction by decomposition according to Proposition 4.12 and Remark 4.13.i). It is clear that these two arrows are inverse to each other.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.15.

Let \(B'\) be the \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(K\) by the indeterminate \(X\) subject to the relations \(\gamma^k(x)=0\) for \(k>n\), i.e. \(B'=K[X,\ldots,\gamma^nX]\) (4.9). Then the \(\lambda\)-ring filtration of \(B'\) is identical to the filtration by the weight with respect to the \(\gamma^jX\).

Taking into account Proposition 4.12, Remark 4.13.ii) and the fact that \(A=K\), \(\operatorname{Fil}^kB'=K.M_k+B'.M_{k+1}\), hence \(\operatorname{Fil}^kB'\) is the ideal generated by the monomials of weight \(\geq k\) with respect to the \(\gamma^jX\).
5. The \(\lambda^p(N,x)\).
This paragraph will only be used in Exp. VII 4, for the study of regular immersions; it may therefore be omitted on first reading.

Let \(d\) be an integer \(\geq1\). We denote by \(A\) the \(\mathbb Z\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra generated by the generators \(N,x\) subject to the relations \(\lambda^i(N)=0\) for \(i>d\); we therefore have:
\[A=\mathbb Z[N,\ldots,\lambda^dN\ ;\ x,\ldots,\lambda^jx,\ldots].\]
We set \(\lambda_{-1}(N)=\sum_{i=0}^d(-1)^i\lambda^i(N)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.

For every \(p\geq1\), \(\lambda^p(x.\lambda_{-1}(N))\) is divisible by \(\lambda_{-1}(N)\).

We know (2.3) that \(\lambda^p(x.\lambda_{-1}(N))=P_p(\lambda^ix,\lambda^j(\lambda_{-1}(N)))\) is an isobaric polynomial of weight \(p\) with respect to the \(\lambda^j(\lambda_{-1}(N))\); it therefore suffices to show the lemma for the \(\lambda^p(\lambda_{-1}(N))\).

Let \(B\) be the \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(\mathbb Z\) by the indeterminates \(N_1,\ldots,N_d\), subject to the relations \(\lambda^i(N_j)=0\) if \(i\geq2\). It is clear that \(\lambda^k(\sum_{i=1}^dN_i)=0\) for \(k>d\). We can therefore define a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism \(f\) from \(A\) to \(B\), by sending \(N\) to \(\sum_{i=1}^dN_i\); we then have \(\widehat G(f)(\lambda_t(N))=\prod_{i=1}^d\lambda_t(N_i)=\prod_{i=1}^d(1+N_it)\), which shows that for \(1\leq k\leq d\), \(\lambda^kN\) is sent to the \(k\)-th symmetric function of the \(N_i\), and consequently that \(f\) is injective and identifies \(A\) with the ring of symmetric polynomials in the \(N_i\).

We then have \(\lambda_{-1}(N)=\prod_{i=1}^d(1-N_i)\), and \(\lambda_t(\lambda_{-1}(N))=\lambda_t(\prod_{i=1}^d(1-N_i))\). The \(\lambda^p(\lambda_{-1}(N))\) are polynomials with respect to the \(N_i\); if for some index \(i\) we set \(N_i=1\), we see that \(\lambda_t(\lambda_{-1}(N))\) becomes \(\lambda_t(0)=1\), hence that the \(\lambda^p(\lambda_{-1}(N))\) vanish for \(N_i=1\), hence that they contain \(1-N_i\) as a factor. Hence they contain the product of the \(1-N_i\) as a factor, i.e. they are divisible by \(\lambda_{-1}(N)\) in \(B\), hence also in \(A\), the quotient of two symmetric polynomials being obviously symmetric.
Since \(A\) is an integral domain, we define without ambiguity an element \(\lambda^p(N,x)\) by setting (\(p\geq1\)):
\[(5.3.1)\qquad \lambda^p(N,x).\lambda_{-1}(N)=\lambda^p(x.\lambda_{-1}(N)).\]

The element \(\lambda^p(N,x)\) is therefore a polynomial with respect to the \(\lambda^i(x)\), \(\lambda^j(N)\), isobaric of weight \(p\) with respect to the \(\lambda^i(x)\), since the same is true of \(\lambda^p(x.\lambda_{-1}(N))\) by 2.3.

Let \(d'\) be an integer \(\geq d\), and \(A'\) the \(\mathbb Z\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra generated by the generators \(N'\) and \(x'\) subject to the relations \(\lambda^i(N')=0\) for \(i>d'\); by Proposition 4.7, there exists one and only one \(\lambda\)-homomorphism \(f:A'\longrightarrow A\), sending \(N'\) to \(N\) and \(x'\) to \(x\). From the definition (5.3.1) it follows that \(f(\lambda^p(N',x'))=\lambda^p(N,x)\) for every \(p\geq1\).

If \(B\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring, and \(N''\) an element of \(B\) such that \(\lambda^k(N'')=0\) for \(k>d\), one can, for every \(x''\in B\), define an element \(\lambda^p(N'',x'')\) by substitution, or again by using the unique \(\lambda\)-homomorphism from \(A\) to \(B\) which sends \(N\) to \(N''\), and \(x\) to \(x''\); by the preceding remark, the element obtained does not depend on the integer \(d\) such that \(\lambda^k(N'')=0\) for \(k>d\). Using the same homomorphism, we see that the relation (5.3.1) is satisfied by \(N''\) and \(x''\).
Let us consider the \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(\mathbb Z\) by the indeterminates \(N,x,y\), subject to the relations \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\). From (5.3.1) it follows that:

\[ \begin{aligned} \lambda^p(N,x+y).\lambda_{-1}(N) &=\lambda^p((x+y).\lambda_{-1}(N))\\ &=\sum_{i+j=p}\lambda^i(x.\lambda_{-1}(N)).\lambda^j(y.\lambda_{-1}(N))\\ &=\sum_{\substack{i+j=p\\i,j\ne0}}\lambda^i(N,x).\lambda^j(N,y).(\lambda_{-1}(N))^2 +(\lambda^p(N,x)+\lambda^p(N,y))\lambda_{-1}(N). \end{aligned} \]

Since the ring under consideration is an integral domain, we deduce from this the following relation, valid by substitution in every \(\lambda\)-ring, and for every element \(N\) such that \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\):
\[(5.4.1)\qquad \lambda^p(N,x+y)=\sum_{\substack{i+j=p\\i,j\ne0}}\lambda^i(N,x).\lambda^j(N,y).\lambda_{-1}(N)+\lambda^p(N,x)+\lambda^p(N,y).\]

Let us now consider the \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(\mathbb Z\) by indeterminates \(N,N',x\) subject to the relations \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\), and \(\lambda^k(N')=0\) for \(k>d'\). We then have \(\lambda^k(N+N')=0\) for \(k>d+d'\); we can therefore define the \(\lambda^p(N+N',x)\). We obtain:
\[ \lambda^p(N+N',x).\lambda_{-1}(N+N')=\lambda^p(x.\lambda_{-1}(N+N')). \]
By additivity of \(\lambda_t\), we have: \(\lambda_{-1}(N+N')=\lambda_{-1}(N).\lambda_{-1}(N')\). From this we deduce:
\[ \begin{aligned} \lambda^p(N+N',x).\lambda_{-1}(N).\lambda_{-1}(N') &=\lambda^p(x.\lambda_{-1}(N).\lambda_{-1}(N'))\\ &=\lambda^p(N',x.\lambda_{-1}(N)).\lambda_{-1}(N'). \end{aligned} \]

Since the ring under consideration is an integral domain, we deduce from this the following relation, valid by substitution in every \(\lambda\)-ring, and for all elements \(N,N'\) such that \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\), \(\lambda^k(N')=0\) for \(k>d'\);
\[(5.4.2)\qquad \lambda^p(N+N',x).\lambda_{-1}(N)=\lambda^p(N',x.\lambda_{-1}(N)).\]
Now let \(A\) be an arbitrary \(\lambda\)-ring, and \(N\) an element of \(A\) such that \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\). We shall associate to \(A\) a new \(\lambda\)-ring \(A_N\).
We first define a new multiplication on \(A\) by setting for \(x,y\in A\):
\[(5.5.1)\qquad x\mathbin{\mathop{.}\limits_N}y=x.y.\lambda_{-1}(N).\]

The ring \(A\) endowed with this multiplication is no longer unital; we therefore consider the ring \(\mathbb Z\times A\), obtained by adjoining a unit to \(A\). Its operations are therefore defined by:
\[(5.5.2)\qquad \begin{aligned} (n,x)+(n',x')&=(n+n',x+x')\\ (n,x).(n',x')&=(nn',nx'+n'x+x\mathbin{\mathop{.}\limits_N}y) \end{aligned}\]
We regard \(A\) as embedded in this ring by \(x\rightsquigarrow(0,x)\). We then define a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure by:
\[(5.5.3)\qquad \lambda_t(n,x)=(1+t)^n.\sum_{p=0}^\infty\lambda^p(N,x).t^p.\]

It is a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure by virtue of (5.4.1). We shall denote by \(A_N\) the pre-\(\lambda\)-ring thus obtained. To see that it is a \(\lambda\)-ring, it suffices by substitution to carry out the proof in the case where \(A\) is the \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(\mathbb Z\) by the generators \(N,X,Y\), subject to the relations \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\). We then define a homomorphism \(A_N\longrightarrow A\), by sending \((1,0)\) to 1, and \((0,x)\) to \(x.\lambda_{-1}(N)\). It is immediate to see from (5.5.1) and (5.3.1) that it is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, and \(A\) being an integral domain in the case under consideration, one checks at once that it is injective; since \(A\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring, we deduce the result.
In the \(\lambda\)-ring \(A_N\), we can define operations \(\gamma\) (3.2). For \(x\in\Lambda\), the value of \(\gamma^p\) computed on the element \((0,x)\) of \(A_N\) is given by:
\[\gamma^p(0,x)=\sum_{i=0}^{p-1}\binom{p-1}i\lambda^i(N,x),\]
it is therefore an element of \(A\); it is defined by the relation:
\[(5.5.4)\qquad \gamma^p(N,x).\lambda_{-1}(N)=\gamma^p(x.\lambda_{-1}(N)),\]
and the universal polynomials which result from it.
