Let \(A\) be the \(\lambda\)-ring generated over \(\mathbb Z\) by the generators \(N,L,x\), subject to the relations \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\), \(\lambda^k(L)=0\) for \(k>1\). We propose to compute the \(\lambda^p(N+L,x)\) in terms of the \(\lambda^q(N,x)\).
By definition:
\[\lambda^p(N+L,x).\lambda_{-1}(N).\lambda_{-1}(L)=\lambda^p(x.\lambda_{-1}(N).\lambda_{-1}(L)),\ \text{soit :}\]

\[ \begin{aligned} 1+\lambda_{-1}(N).\lambda_{-1}(L).\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(N+L,x).t^k &=\lambda_t(x.\lambda_{-1}(N))\circ\lambda_t(1-L)\\ &=\frac{\lambda_t(x.\lambda_{-1}(N))}{\lambda_{Lt}(x.\lambda_{-1}(N))} \end{aligned}\]
\(\left(\text{car }\lambda_t(1-L)=\dfrac{\lambda_t(1)}{\lambda_t(L)}=\dfrac{1+t}{1+Lt}\right)\) and we apply (2.3.2))

\[ \begin{aligned} &=\frac{1+\cdots+\lambda^k(x.\lambda_{-1}(N))t^k+\cdots} {1+\cdots+\lambda^k(x.\lambda_{-1}(N))L^kt^k+\cdots}\\ &=1+\frac{\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(N,x).\lambda_{-1}(N).(1-L^k)t^k} {1+\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(N,x).\lambda_{-1}(N).L^kt^k} \end{aligned}\]
Hence \(\lambda^p(N+L,x)\) is the coefficient of \(t^p\) in the series:
\[\frac{\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(N,x)(1+\cdots+L^{k-1})t^k} {1+\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(N,x)\lambda_{-1}(N)L^kt^k}.\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.7.

Let \(K\) be a binomial ring, \(A\) an augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra, and \(N\) an element of \(A\) such that \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\). Then for every \(x\in A\) and every \(p\geq1\), we have:

\[(5.7.1)\qquad\gamma^p(N,x)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{p-d}A.\]

Let \(A_0\) be the \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra generated by generators \(N'_0,X'_0\), subject to the relations \(\gamma^k(N'_0)=0\) for \(k>d\). We define the augmentation homomorphism \(A_0\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\varepsilon}}K\) by \(\varepsilon(N'_0)=\varepsilon(X'_0)=0\). We know that \(A_0\) is the ring of polynomials with coefficients in \(K\) with respect to the indeterminates \(\gamma^i(N'_0)\), with \(1\leq i\leq d\), and \(\gamma^j(X'_0)\), with \(j\geq1\), and that the \(\lambda\)-filtration on \(A_0\) is identical to the filtration by the weight, assigning the weight \(i\) to \(\gamma^i(N'_0)\), \(\gamma^j(X'_0)\) (4.12 and 4.15).
Let us set \(N_0=N'_0+d\), \(X_0=X'_0+\varepsilon(x)\). We shall first show 5.7 for \(N_0\) and \(X_0\). By what precedes, (5.7.1) is equivalent to:
\[\gamma^p(N_0,X_0).\gamma^d(N'_0)\in\operatorname{Fil}^pA_0.\]
Now \(\gamma^d(N'_0)=(-1)^d\lambda_{-1}(N_0)\); this relation can therefore be written:
\[(-1)^d\gamma^p(X_0\,\gamma^d(N_0))\in\operatorname{Fil}^pA_0\]
by (5.5.4). This relation is indeed satisfied, since \(\gamma^d(N_0)\) has zero augmentation.

By 4.7, there exists a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-homomorphism \(A_0\longrightarrow A\) compatible with the augmentations sending \(N'_0\) to \(N-d\), and \(X'_0\) to \(x-\varepsilon(x)\), hence \(N_0\) to \(N\) and \(X_0\) to \(x\), whence the result, the homomorphism being compatible with the filtrations.
6. Chern ring

In this paragraph, the base ring will be a binomial ring (2.7) \(K\), fixed once and for all. We shall use the category \(\mathcal C\) (1.1), and consequently return to the notations of paragraph 1, reserving the notation \(\widehat G\) for the functor defined on \(\mathcal C\) (1.1), and denoting by \(\widehat G\circ i\) its restriction to \(\mathcal C_0\) (1.8).
We have defined in 3.5 and 3.6 morphisms of functors:

\[ \begin{aligned} (\widehat G\circ i)\times(\widehat G\circ i)&\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\nabla}}\widehat G\circ i,\\ \widehat G^t\circ i&\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\gamma'}}(\widehat G^u\circ i)\circ(\widehat G^t\circ i)_\nabla,\\ \widehat G\circ i&\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{a}}\widehat G\circ i, \end{aligned}\]
the last being multiplication for the law \(\nabla\) by \(\gamma_t(a)\), with \(a\in K\).
By 1.9, we deduce from these morphisms:

\[ \begin{aligned} \widehat G\times\widehat G&\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\nabla}}\widehat G_g,\\ \widehat G^t&\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\gamma'}}\widehat G^u\circ i\circ\widehat G^t_{g\,\nabla},\\ \widehat G&\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{a}}\widehat G_g\ ; \end{aligned}\]

and it follows from the isobarity properties of the polynomials \(P'_n\), \(Q'_{i,j}\), \(R_{n,a}\) (3.5, 3.6, 2.9) that the image of the first and third functors is in \(\widehat G\), and that of the second in the subgroup of formal power series in \(u\) whose coefficients in nonzero degree are in \(\widehat G^t\).

By composing the first and the third with the canonical morphism: \(\widehat G\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}.}^+\) (1.11), we obtain by 1.12 two morphisms of functors on \(\mathcal C_g\):

\[ \begin{aligned} (1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+)\times(1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+)&\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{*}}1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+,\\ 1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+&\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{a}}1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+, \end{aligned}\]

defining on the functor \(1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+\) a multiplication and operations of \(K\). By 1.13, the multiplication is defined by its value on the element \((1+X,1+Y)\) of \((1+\widehat{K[X,Y]}^+)^2\); it will be denoted \(*\); we therefore have:
\[(6.1.1)\qquad(1+X)*(1+Y)=\frac{1+X+Y}{(1+X)(1+Y)}.\]
The \(n\)-th universal polynomial of this law is the isobaric component of weight \(n\) of \(P'_n\) (1.15) and will be denoted \(P''_n\).

The operations of \(K\) will again be denoted by the exponential (3.8). The universal polynomials defining these operations are the isobaric components of lowest degree of the \(R_{n,a}\) (1.15); the latter being isobaric, they are the \(R_{n,a}\) themselves.

Let \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C_g)\); the ring \(1+\widehat A^+\) defined by the preceding laws is in general without unit element; we therefore extend this ring by adjunction of the scalars of \(K\), by introducing the new ring \(K\times(1+\widehat A^+)\), endowed with the composition laws defined by: \(\forall a,a'\in A\), \(\varphi,\varphi'\in1+\widehat A^+\),
\[(6.1.2)\qquad \begin{aligned} (a,\varphi)+(a',\varphi')&=(a+a',\varphi\varphi')\ ;\\ (a,\varphi).(a',\varphi')&=(aa',\varphi^{a'}.\varphi'^{\,a}.(\varphi*\varphi')). \end{aligned}\]

The ring thus constructed admits (1.1) as unit element, and \(1+\widehat A^+\) is identified with the ideal \((0)\times(1+\widehat A^+)\). It is naturally endowed with a \(K\)-algebra structure, such that:
\[(6.1.3)\qquad a.(a',\varphi)=(a,1).(a',\varphi)=(aa',\varphi^a).\]

We endow it with a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra structure in the following way: we remarked above that the image of the functor \(\gamma':\widehat G^t\longrightarrow\widehat G^u\circ i\circ\widehat G^t_g\) is in the subgroup of formal power series in \(u\) whose coefficients in nonzero degree are in \(\widehat G^t\). Using the canonical morphism: \(\widehat G\longrightarrow1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}.}^+\), which gives: \(\widehat G\longrightarrow K\times(1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}.}^+)\), we finally deduce a morphism:
\[\widehat G^t\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\gamma'}}\widehat G^u\circ i\circ(K\times(1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}.}^+)),\]
which by 1.12 defines a morphism of functors on:
\[1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{'}}\widehat G^u\circ i\circ(K\times(1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+)).\]

The universal polynomials defining this morphism are the coefficients of the \(\gamma'^i(1+X_1+\cdots+X_n+\cdots)\); the \(j\)-th coefficient is the isobaric component of weight \(j\) of \(Q'_{i,j}\) (proof analogous to that of 1.15), and will be denoted \(Q''_{i,j}\).

We finally extend the morphism thus constructed to \(K\times(1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+)\) in the natural way, using the \(\lambda\)-structure of \(K\). We therefore obtain a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring structure on \(K\times(1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+)\), defined by the corresponding operations \(\gamma\), which are such that:
\[(6.1.4)\qquad \gamma_t(a,\varphi)=\gamma_t(a).\Bigl(1+\sum_{p\geq1}\gamma'^p(\varphi)t^p\Bigr),\]
and which will be denoted \(\gamma\) instead of \(\gamma'\), no confusion being possible.

To check that it is a \(\lambda\)-ring structure, we check the commutativity of (3.7.2); it is known for the elements of \(K\); for the elements of \(1+\widehat{\vphantom{X}.}^+\), it follows from that of (3.7.2) for \(\widehat G\) by passage to the quotient. From this we obtain the result at once.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.2.

The functor defined in 6.1 on the category of graded \(K\)-algebras, with values in the category of \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebras, is called the Chern ring; its value for \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal C_g)\) is denoted \(\operatorname{Ch}(A)\) (or, if there are risks of confusion, \(\operatorname{Ch}_K(A)\)).
From formulas (6.1.1), (6.1.2), (6.1.4), and (3.6.2), we deduce the relations:
\[(6.2.1)\qquad \begin{aligned} (1,1+X).(1,1+Y)&=(1,1+X+Y)\\ \lambda^i(1,1+X)&=0\quad\text{pour }i\geq2, \end{aligned}\]
relations which served to define the Chern ring in [1]. From these formulas, one can conversely recover the relations (6.1.1) and (6.1.4).

Let us indicate the reason for which the Chern ring is introduced. We place ourselves in the situation of example 3.9, and we denote by \(A\) the graded object associated (by the filtration defined in 3.10) to the \(K'\) of a commutatively locally ringed topos (which we suppose to be a \(\lambda\)-ring for simplicity); \(K\) is the augmentation ring. We shall see later that one can define, for every element of an augmented \(\lambda\)-ring, Chern classes with values in the associated graded object (6.7). To every locally free sheaf of finite type, we can therefore associate an element of \(\operatorname{Ch}(A)\), whose component on the factor \(K\) is the rank of the sheaf, and whose component on the factor \(1+\widehat A^+\) is the sum of the Chern classes. Formulas (6.2.1) are then the usual formulas giving the Chern classes and the rank of the tensor product of two invertible sheaves and of the exterior powers of an invertible sheaf (that is to say, the map just defined from the \(K'\) to \(\operatorname{Ch}(A)\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism).

For every graded \(K\)-algebra \(A\), we denote by \(A_{\mathbb Q}\) the \(K\)-algebra \(A\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\), graded in the obvious way. By 1.13, we define a functorial homomorphism: \(1+\widehat A^+\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\operatorname{ch}}}\widehat A_{\mathbb Q}^+\) by giving its value for \(1+X\), namely
\[(6.3.1)\qquad\operatorname{ch}(1+X)=\exp(X)-1.\]

This homomorphism is compatible with the multiplication defined in 6.1 on \(1+\widehat A^+\): by 1.13 it suffices to show this for the elements \(1+X\), \(1+Y\) of \(1+\widehat{K[X,Y]}^+\); now we have:

\[ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{ch}((1+X)*(1+Y)) &=\operatorname{ch}\left(\frac{1+X+Y}{(1+X)(1+Y)}\right)\\ &=\operatorname{ch}(1+X+Y)-\operatorname{ch}(1+X)-\operatorname{ch}(1+Y)\\ &=\exp(X+Y)-\exp(X)-\exp(Y)+1\\ &=\operatorname{ch}(1+X).\operatorname{ch}(1+Y). \end{aligned}\]
Let us denote by \(\eta\) the endomorphism (functorial with respect to \(A\)) of \(\widehat A_{\mathbb Q}^+\), defined by:
\[\eta\Bigl(\sum_{k\geq1}a_kt^k\Bigr)=\sum_{k\geq1}\frac{(-1)^{k-1}}{(k-1)!}.a_kt^k.\]
Then, \(\forall\varphi\in1+\widehat A^+\),
\[(6.3.2)\qquad\operatorname{ch}(\varphi)=\eta(\operatorname{Log}\varphi).\]

Indeed, \(\operatorname{ch}\) and \(\eta\circ\operatorname{Log}\) are two functorial homomorphisms of abelian groups, and for them to be equal it suffices that they coincide on \(1+X\), which is obvious.
One deduces from this that \(\operatorname{ch}\) commutes with the operations of \(K\): indeed, it suffices here again to show it for \(1+X\), and we have:

\[ \begin{aligned} \forall a\in K,\quad\operatorname{ch}((1+X)^a) &=\operatorname{ch}(\exp(a\operatorname{Log}(1+X)))\\ &=\eta(a\operatorname{Log}(1+X))\\ &=a.\eta(\operatorname{Log}(1+X))\\ &=a.\operatorname{ch}(1+X). \end{aligned}\]
One can therefore in an obvious way extend \(\operatorname{ch}\) to a homomorphism of \(K\)-algebras: \(K\times(1+\widehat A^+)\longrightarrow K\oplus\widehat A_{\mathbb Q}^+\). We therefore have:
\[(6.3.3)\qquad\operatorname{ch}(a,\varphi)=a+\eta(\operatorname{Log}\varphi).\]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.4.

The homomorphism \(\operatorname{ch}:\operatorname{Ch}A\longrightarrow K\oplus\widehat A_{\mathbb Q}^+\) which has just been defined is called the Chern character.

If \(A\) is of characteristic 0 (i.e. is a \(\mathbb Q\)-algebra), the Chern character is an isomorphism: indeed, let \(\psi\in K\oplus\widehat A^+\) ; one can write \(\psi=a+\psi_1\), where \(\psi_1\) has no term of degree zero ; one then defines a morphism inverse to \(\operatorname{ch}\) by \(\psi\rightsquigarrow(a,\exp(\eta^{-1}(\psi_1)))\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.5.

Let \(A\) be a graded \(K\)-algebra ; consider on \(\operatorname{Ch}(A)\) the filtration defined as follows (filtration by degree):

\[(6.5.1)\qquad (\operatorname{Ch}(A))_0=\operatorname{Ch}(A);\quad\forall n\geq1,\ (\operatorname{Ch}(A))_n=0\times1+\widehat A^{\geq n},\]

where \(\widehat A^{\geq n}=\prod_{i\geq n}A^i\). We have the following congruences:

i) Let \(n\geq1\), and \(\varphi\in(\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\) ; set \(\varphi=1+a_n+\cdots\) ; then:

\[(6.5.2)\qquad\operatorname{ch}(\varphi)=\frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}.a_n+\cdots\ ;\]

ii) Let \(\varphi\in(\operatorname{Ch}A)_m\), \(\psi\in(\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\) ; set \(\varphi=1+a_m+\cdots\), \(\psi=1+b_n+\cdots\) ; then:

\[(6.5.3)\qquad \varphi*\psi=1-\frac{(m+n-1)!}{(m-1)!(n-1)!}.a_mb_n+\cdots,\]

and consequently, the filtration by degree is a ring filtration.

i) The Chern character is by construction a morphism of filtered functors ; from the congruence \(\varphi\equiv1+a_n\pmod{(\operatorname{Ch}A)_{n+1}}\), we therefore deduce \(\operatorname{ch}(\varphi)\equiv\operatorname{ch}(1+a_n)\pmod{\widehat A^{\geq n+1}}\) ; now
\[\operatorname{ch}(1+a_n)=\eta(\operatorname{Log}(1+a_n))=\frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}a_n+\cdots\]

ii) By substitution, it suffices to prove formula (6.5.3) for \(A=K[X_i;Y_j]_{i,j\in\mathbb N}\) and for \(\varphi=1+\sum_{k\geq m}X_k\), \(\psi=1+\sum_{k\geq n}Y_k\). Since \(K\) is torsion-free, one can embed this ring in its tensor product with \(\mathbb Q\), and one thus sees that \(\operatorname{ch}\) is injective. Set \(\varphi*\psi=1+Z_p+\cdots\), \(Z_p\) being the first nonzero term and \(\neq1\) that of \(\varphi*\psi\). The multiplicativity of \(\operatorname{ch}\) gives us, by (6.5.2):
\[1+\frac{(-1)^{p-1}}{(p-1)!}.Z_p+\cdots=\Bigl(1+\frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{(m-1)!}.X_m+\cdots\Bigr)\Bigl(1+\frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}.Y_n+\cdots\Bigr),\]
whence the value of \(Z_p\) by identifying the terms of lowest degree.

The \(\lambda\)-ring \(\operatorname{Ch}A\) is augmented to \(K\): indeed, one defines a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism from \(\operatorname{Ch}A\) to \(K\) by \((a,\varphi)\rightsquigarrow a\). It is therefore endowed with a canonical filtration of augmented \(\lambda\)-ring. We shall compare it with the one which has just been defined.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.6.1.

Let \(x\in\operatorname{Ch}A\) be an element of the form \(x=(n,1+\cdots+a_n)\). Then:

i) \(\lambda^i(x)=0\) for \(i\geq n\) ;

ii) \(\lambda^n(x)=(1,1+a_1)\)

iii) \(\sum_{i=0}^n(-1)^i\lambda^i(x)=0,1-(n-1)!a_n+\cdots)\), the unwritten terms being of degree \(>n\).

By functoriality, it suffices to give the proof in the case where \(A\) is the ring \(K[X_1,\ldots,X_n]\), filtered by the weight with respect to the \(X_i\), \(X_i\) being of weight \(i\), and for the element \((n,1+X_1+\cdots+X_n)\).
One embeds \(A\) into \(A'=K[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\) by means of the symmetric functions, and thus obtains an injective homomorphism: \(\operatorname{Ch}A\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}A'\) ; we then get:
\[ \begin{aligned} (n,1+X_1+\cdots+X_n)&=\Bigl(n,\prod_{i=1}^n(1+T_i)\Bigr)\\ &=\sum_{i=1}^n(1,1+T_i) \end{aligned}\]
One deduces from this, by (6.2.1):
\[\lambda_t(x)=\prod_{i=1}^n(1+(1,1+T_i)t),\ \text{et on en tire :}\]
\[\lambda^k(x)=0\ \text{pour }k>n,\quad\text{et}\quad \lambda^n(x)=\prod_{i=1}^n(1,1+T_i)=\Bigl(1,1+\sum_{i=1}^nT_i\Bigr)=(1,1+X_1)\]
that is: \(\lambda_{-1}(x)=(0,1-(n-1)!X_n+\cdots)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.6.2.

The relation 6.6.1.iii) is true for every element \(X\) of augmentation \(n\).
To prove this we shall use the lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.6.3.

For every \(n\), \((\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\) is a \(\lambda\)-ideal of \(\operatorname{Ch}A\).

We may assume \(n\geq1\). Let \(x=(0,\varphi)\in(\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\) ; one can write \(\gamma^i(0,\varphi)=(0,1+Q''_{i,1}+\cdots+Q''_{i,j}+\cdots)\), the \(Q''_{i,j}\) being the isobaric components of weight \(j\) of the \(Q'_{i,j}\) (3.6). For every \(j\), the polynomials \(Q''_{i,j}\) are therefore polynomials in the first \(j\) coefficients of \(\varphi\). If \(j<n\), they are therefore zero, and consequently the \(\gamma^i(x)\) belong to \((\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\), and \((\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\) is a \(\lambda\)-ideal.

Now let \(x=(n,1+a_1+\cdots+a_n+\cdots)\) be an element of augmentation \(n\) of \(\operatorname{Ch}A\) ; \(x\) is congruent mod. \((\operatorname{Ch}A)_{n+1}\) to \((n,1+\cdots+a_n)\), and consequently \(\lambda_{-1}(x)\) is congruent to \(\lambda_{-1}(n,1+\cdots+a_n)=(0,1-(n-1)!a_n+\cdots)\), whence:
\[\lambda_{-1}(x)=(0,1-(n-1)!a_n+\cdots).\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.6.4.

Let \(\varepsilon\) be the augmentation homomorphism of \(\operatorname{Ch}A\) ; then, for every \(x\in\operatorname{Ch}A\), we have:

\[\gamma^n(x-\varepsilon(x))=(0,1+(-1)^{n-1}(n-1)!a_n+\cdots)\]

By definition, \(\gamma^n(x-\varepsilon(x))=(-1)^n\sum_{i=0}^n(-1)^i\lambda(x+n-\varepsilon(x))\). Since \(x+n-\varepsilon(x)\) is of augmentation \(n\), we can apply corollary 6.6.2 to it, whence the result.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.6.5.

With the notations of 3.10, \(\operatorname{Fil}^n\operatorname{Ch}A\subset(\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\).

By the preceding corollary, \(\gamma^n(x-\varepsilon(x))\in(\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\). The corollary then follows from the fact that the two filtrations are ring filtrations, and from the fact that the \(\gamma^n(x-\varepsilon(x))\), for variable \(n\) and \(x\), generate the \(\lambda\)-ring filtration.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.6.7.

Let \(A\) be a graded \(K\)-algebra of characteristic zero, with bounded degrees ; then the two filtrations defined on \(\operatorname{Ch}A\) are identical.

Suppose that \(\operatorname{Fil}^pA=0\), and let \(x=(0,1+a_n+\cdots+a_p)\) be an element of \((\operatorname{Ch}A)_n\). Set \(x'=(0,1+\frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}a_n+a_{n+1}+\cdots+a_p)\) ; we then have: \(\gamma^n(x')=(0,1+a_n+\cdots+a'_p)\), since \(x'\) has zero augmentation. Consequently, \(x-\gamma^n(x')\in(\operatorname{Ch}A)_{n+1}\). Iterating the operation \(p-n\) times, we arrive at \((\operatorname{Ch}A)_{p+1}\), which is zero, and we have thus written \(x\) as an element of \(\operatorname{Fil}^n\operatorname{Ch}A\). Taking (6.6.5) into account, the two filtrations are therefore identical.

We now assume that \(A\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring augmented to \(K\), the augmentation homomorphism being denoted \(\varepsilon\). We thus have on \(A\) the canonical filtration of augmented \(\lambda\)-ring, and we can therefore associate to \(\operatorname{Gr}A\) its Chern ring.

If \(x\in A\), then \(\gamma_t(x-\varepsilon(x))\in\widehat G(A)\) by definition of the filtration of augmented \(\lambda\)-ring. We write \(c(x)\) and call the image of \(\gamma_t(x-\varepsilon(x))\) in \(\widehat G(A)/\widehat G'(A)=1+\widehat{\operatorname{Gr}A}^+\) the total Chern class of \(x\) ; we write \(c^i(x)\), and call the \(i\)-th coefficient of \(c(x)\) the \(i\)-th Chern class of \(x\) ; we thus have:
\[(6.7.1)\qquad c^i(x)=\operatorname{cl}_{\operatorname{Gr}^iA}(\gamma^i(x-\varepsilon(x)))\ ;\quad c(x)=1+c^1(x)+\cdots\]
We then set:
\[(6.7.2)\qquad\widetilde c(x)=(\varepsilon(x),c(x)),\]
and we call \(\widetilde c(x)\) the completed Chern class of \(x\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.8.

The completed Chern class \(\widetilde c:A\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}A)\) is a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-homomorphism of augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebras.
It is clear that the homomorphism thus defined is additive ; to check that it is multiplicative, one writes:

\[ \begin{aligned} \gamma_t(xy-\varepsilon(xy)) &=\gamma_t(\varepsilon(y)(x-\varepsilon(x))).\gamma_t(\varepsilon(x)(y-\varepsilon(y))).\gamma_t((x-\varepsilon(x))(y-\varepsilon(y)))\\ &=(\gamma_t(x-\varepsilon(x)))^{\varepsilon(y)}.(\gamma_t(y-\varepsilon(y)))^{\varepsilon(x)}.(\gamma_t(x-\varepsilon(x))\nabla\gamma_t(y-\varepsilon(y))) \end{aligned}\]
whence the result by (6.1.2) on passing to the quotient.
If \(a\in K\), \(\widetilde c(a)=(a,1)\), so \(\widetilde c\) is a homomorphism of \(K\)-algebras ; moreover it is compatible with the augmentations.
It remains finally to see that it is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism. We must therefore show the commutativity of the diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{ A\ar[r]^{\gamma_t}\ar[d]_{\widetilde c}&\widehat G(A)\ar[d]^{\widehat G_g(\widetilde c)}\\ \operatorname{Ch}A\ar[r]_{\gamma_t}&\widehat G_g(\operatorname{Ch}A) }\]

By additivity, it suffices to show it for the elements of \(K\), for which it is obvious, and for the elements of zero augmentation of \(A\), for which it follows, by the definition of \(c\) and (6.1.4), from that of diagram (3.7.2).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.9.

Let \(A\) be an augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra, \(z\in\operatorname{Fil}^mA\) ; then:

\[\gamma^m(z)\equiv(-1)^{m-1}(m-1)!z\pmod{\operatorname{Fil}^{m+1}A},\]

i.e.

\[c^m(z)=\operatorname{cl}_{\operatorname{Gr}^mA}((-1)^{m-1}(m-1)!\,z).\]
We must see that these two elements have the same image in \(\operatorname{Gr}^mA\), that is, that the \(m\)-th Chern class of \(z\) is the image of \((-1)^{m-1}(m-1)!z\).
One can write \(z\) as a sum \(\sum a.\gamma^{i_1}(x_1)\cdots\gamma^{i_k}(x_k)\), with \(a\in A\), \(\varepsilon(x_i)=0\) for every \(i\), and \(i_1+\cdots+i_k\geq m\). We then have:
\[\widetilde c(z)=\sum\widetilde c(a).\widetilde c(\gamma^{i_1}(x_1))\cdots\widetilde c(\gamma^{i_k}(x_k))\]
Since \(\widetilde c\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, we can write:
\[\widetilde c(\gamma^{i_j}(x_j))=\gamma^{i_j}(\widetilde c(x_j)).\]
Taking into account the definition of \(c\), and (6.6.4), we obtain:
\[\widetilde c(\gamma^{i_j}(x_j))=(0,1+(-1)^{i_j-1}(i_j-1)!\,\gamma^{i_j}(x_j)+\cdots)\]
By (6.5.3), and the fact that \(\widetilde c(a)=(a,1)\), we finally obtain:

\[ \begin{aligned} \widetilde c(z)&=\sum\bigl(0,(1+(-1)^{i_1+\cdots+i_k-1}(i_1+\cdots+i_k-1)!\,\gamma^{i_1}(x_1)\cdots\gamma^{i_k}(x_k)+\cdots)^a\bigr)\\ &=\Bigl(0,1+\sum_{\substack{(x_1,\ldots,x_k)\\i_1+\cdots+i_k=m}}(-1)^{m-1}(m-1)!\,a\,\gamma^{i_1}(x_1)\cdots\gamma^{i_k}(x_k)+\cdots\Bigr)\\ &=(0,1+(-1)^{m-1}(m-1)!\,\bar z+\cdots), \end{aligned}\]
whence the result.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.10.

Let \(K\) be a binomial ring, \(R\) an augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra, \(N\) an element of \(R\) such that \(\lambda^k(N)=0\) for \(k>d\), and \(x\in\operatorname{Fil}^1R\). Then:

\[\gamma^{d+i}(N,x)-(-1)^{d+i-1}(d+i-1)!x\in\operatorname{Fil}^{i+1}R.\]
One can find elements \(z_{\alpha,j}\) \(R\), and \(a_\alpha\in K\) such that:
\[x=\sum_\alpha a_\alpha\gamma^{i_1}(x_{\alpha,1})\cdots\gamma^{i_k}(x_{\alpha,k}),\]

with \(i_1+\cdots+i_k\geq i\), and \(\varepsilon(z_{\alpha,j})=0\) for every \(\alpha\) and every \(j\). Let \(R_0\) be the \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra generated by generators \(N'_0,X_{\alpha,j}\) subject to the relations:
\[\gamma^k(N'_0)=0\quad\text{pour }k>d.\]

One defines an augmentation homomorphism \(R_0\longrightarrow K\) by \(\varepsilon(N'_0)=\varepsilon(X_{\alpha,j})=0\). By (4.9.1), \(R_0\) is the ring of polynomials with coefficients in \(K\) in the indeterminates \(\gamma^n(N'_0)\), with \(1\leq n\leq d\), and \(\gamma^p(X_{\alpha,j})\) with \(p\geq1\), and the \(\lambda\)-filtration of \(R_0\) is identical to its filtration by weight, assigning the weight \(k\) to \(\gamma^k(N'_0)\) and \(\gamma^k(X_{\alpha,j})\) (4.12 and 4.15).
We set:
\[X_0=\sum_\alpha a_\alpha\gamma^{i_1}(X_{\alpha,1})\cdots\gamma^{i_k}(X_{\alpha,k}),\]

and we shall prove 6.10 first for \(X_0\) and \(N'_0=N_0+d\) (which indeed satisfy the hypotheses of 6.10). Taking into account the remark made above on the filtration of \(R_0\), it suffices for this to show that:
\[\gamma^{d+i}(N_0,X_0)\gamma^d(N'_0)-(-1)^{d+i-1}(d+i-1)!X_0\gamma^d(N'_0)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{d+i+1}R_0.\]
Now we have \(\lambda_{-1}(N_0)=(-1)^d\gamma^d(N_0)\) ; this relation can thus be written:
\[(-1)^d\gamma^{d+i}(X_0\,(-1)^d\gamma^d(N'_0))-(-1)^{d+i-1}(d+i-1)!X_0\gamma^d(N'_0)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{d+i+1}R_0.\]
Set \(z=(-1)^dX_0\gamma^d(N'_0)\) ; then \(z\in\operatorname{Fil}^{d+i}R_0\), and we are reduced to showing:
\[\gamma^{d+i}(z)-(-1)^{d+i-1}(d+i-1)!z\in\operatorname{Fil}^{d+i+1}R_0.\]
Now this follows from 6.9.

To deduce 6.10 from this special case, one considers the \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-homomorphism \(R_0\longrightarrow R\) which sends \(N'_0\) to \(N-d\) and \(X_{\alpha,j}\) to \(x_{\alpha,j}\) for every \(\alpha\) and every \(j\) (4.7). This homomorphism is compatible with the \(\lambda\)-filtrations, and sends \(N_0\) to \(N\), and \(X_0\) to \(X\), whence the result.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.11.

We assume that \(K\) is of characteristic zero. Let \(n\geq0\) be an integer. Then the functor \(\operatorname{Ch}\) is an equivalence of categories from the category of graded \(K\)-algebras \(\Lambda\) such that \(\Lambda_0=K\), \(\Lambda_k=0\) for \(k>n\), to the category of augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebras \(\Lambda\) such that \(\operatorname{Fil}^{n+1}\Lambda=0\) ; a quasi-inverse functor is given by \(\Lambda\rightsquigarrow\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda\).

Let \(\Lambda\) be a graded \(K\)-algebra such that \(\Lambda_k=0\) for \(k>n\), endowed with the filtration associated with its grading ; then \((\operatorname{Ch}\Lambda)_{n+1}=0\), hence \(\operatorname{Fil}^{n+1}\operatorname{Ch}\Lambda=0\) (6.6.5). Conversely, if \(\Lambda\) is an augmented \(K\)-algebra such that \(\operatorname{Fil}^{n+1}\Lambda=0\), then \(\operatorname{Gr}^0\Lambda=K\), \(\operatorname{Gr}^k\Lambda=0\) for \(k>n\).
We shall define isomorphisms of functors:
\[\Lambda\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}(\operatorname{Ch}\Lambda),\qquad \Lambda\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda).\]

Since \(\Lambda\) has finite grading, the graded objects associated with the filtrations on \(\operatorname{Ch}\Lambda\) of \(\lambda\)-ring and by degree are identical (6.6.7). One then defines \(u:\Lambda\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_{\deg}(\operatorname{Ch}\Lambda)\) by the identity on \(\Lambda_0\) and by:
\[\forall i>1,\ \forall a_i\in\Lambda_i,\quad u(a_i)=\operatorname{cl}_{\operatorname{Gr}^i}(0,1+(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!a_i),\]

and one extends by additivity. By relation (6.5.3), it is a homomorphism of graded rings, and it is easy to see that it is compatible with the operations of \(K\). The inverse morphism \(u^{-1}\) can be defined as follows: \(\forall i\geq1\), \(\forall\bar\varphi\in\operatorname{Gr}^i(\operatorname{Ch}\Lambda)\), the \(i\)-th coefficient \(a_i\) of \(\varphi\) does not depend on the way in which \(\bar\varphi\) has been lifted to \((\operatorname{Ch}\Lambda)_i\), and we set:
\[u^{-1}(\bar\varphi)=\frac{(-1)^{i-1}}{(i-1)!}\,a_i.\]
The homomorphism \(u\) is therefore an isomorphism.

The isomorphism \(\Lambda\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda)\) is defined by the completed Chern class \(\widetilde c\). To check that it indeed defines an isomorphism, it suffices to check that the homomorphism obtained by passing to the associated graded objects is one, the filtrations being discrete. We thus obtain:

\[ \operatorname{Gr}\Lambda\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\operatorname{Gr}(\widetilde c)}} \operatorname{Gr}_\lambda(\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda))\simeq \operatorname{Gr}_{\deg}(\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda)) \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{u^{-1}}}\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda.\]

It suffices to show that the composite is the identity. Since the homomorphisms are homomorphisms of graded \(K\)-algebras, it suffices, by the definition of the \(\lambda\)-ring filtration of \(\Lambda\), to show that it is the identity for the \(\overline{\gamma^i(x)}\), with \(\varepsilon(x)=0\). We then obtain:
\[\operatorname{Gr}(\widetilde c)(\overline{\gamma^i(x)})=\operatorname{cl}_{\operatorname{Gr}^i}(\gamma^i(\widetilde c(x))).\]
Now \(\gamma^i(\widetilde c(x))=(0,1+(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!c^i(x)+\cdots)\), by (6.6.4) ; hence
\[\operatorname{cl}_{\operatorname{Gr}^i}(\gamma^i(\widetilde c(x)))=u(c^i(x)),\]
which shows that \(u^{-1}\circ\operatorname{Gr}(\widetilde c)=\operatorname{Id}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.12.

If \(K\) is of characteristic zero, and \(\Lambda\) is a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra with discrete filtration, then the completed Chern class:

\[\widetilde c:\Lambda\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda)\]

is an isomorphism.
The proof is contained in that of 6.11.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.13.

Let \(A\) be a graded \(K\)-algebra, and \(\Lambda\) an augmented \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra ; we call a Chern theory of \(\Lambda\) with values in \(A\) any \(\lambda\)-homomorphism: \(\Lambda\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(A)\), compatible with the augmentations.

One defines a functor on the category of graded \(K\)-algebras, with values in the category of sets, by associating to every graded \(K\)-algebra \(A\) the set of Chern theories of \(\Lambda\) with values in \(A\), denoted \(\operatorname{Hom}_\lambda(\Lambda,\operatorname{Ch}(A))\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.14.

The functor defined in 6.13 is representable. If \(K\) is of characteristic zero, and \(\Lambda\) has discrete filtration, it is represented by the graded object associated with \(\Lambda\).

Let us first prove the first point. We consider the graded \(K\)-algebra generated by generators \(c^i(x)\), with \(i\geq1\), \(x\in\operatorname{Fil}^1\Lambda\), \(c^i(x)\) being of degree \(i\), and we take its quotient by the following relations:

\[ \begin{aligned} &\text{i)}\quad c^k(x+y)=\sum_{i+j=k}c^i(x)c^j(y),\quad\text{pour }k\geq1,\ x,y\in\operatorname{Fil}^1\Lambda\ ;\\ &\text{ii)}\quad c^k(xy)=P''_k(c^i(x),c^j(y))\ \text{(cf. 6.1)},\quad\text{pour }k\geq1,\ x,y\in\operatorname{Fil}^1\Lambda\ ;\\ &\text{iii)}\quad c^k(ax)=R_{k,a}(c^i(x))\ \text{(cf. 6.1)},\quad\text{pour }k\geq1,\ a\in K,\ x\in\operatorname{Fil}^1\Lambda\ ;\\ &\text{iv)}\quad c^k(\gamma^j(x))=Q''_{j,k}(c^i(x))\ \text{(cf. 6.1)},\quad\text{pour }j,k\geq1,\ x\in\operatorname{Fil}^1\Lambda, \end{aligned}\]
These relations being isobaric, the quotient is a graded \(K\)-algebra \(\Omega\). We define a morphism \(\Lambda\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\widetilde c_0}}\operatorname{Ch}(\Omega)\) by:
\[x\rightsquigarrow(\varepsilon(x),1+c^1(x-\varepsilon(x))+\cdots).\]

The relations imposed show that it is a Chern theory of \(\Lambda\) with values in \(\Omega\). If moreover we have a Chern theory \(f\), with values in a graded \(K\)-algebra \(A\), we have in a unique way a homomorphism: \(\Omega\to A\), such that \(f\) factors through \(\operatorname{Ch}(\Omega)\); it is defined by sending, for every \(i\geq1\) and every \(x\in\operatorname{Fil}^1\Lambda\), the element \(c^i(x)\) to the \(i\)-th coefficient of \(f(x)\) (which is well defined since \(f\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism).

Suppose now that \(K\) has characteristic zero, and that \(\Lambda\) has discrete filtration; let \(n\) be such that \(\operatorname{Fil}^n\Lambda=0\). Then the graded algebra \(\Omega\) is zero in degrees \(\geq n\). To show this, we must show that for \(x_1,\ldots,x_p\in\operatorname{Fil}^1\Lambda\) and \(k_1+\cdots+k_p\geq n\), we have:
\[c^{k_1}(x_1)\cdots c^{k_p}(x_p)=0.\]
Now by hypothesis, we have:
\[\gamma^{k_1}(x_1)\cdots\gamma^{k_p}(x_p)=0\ ;\]
Applying the \(\lambda\)-homomorphism \(\widetilde c_0\), we obtain:
\[\gamma^{k_1}(\widetilde c_0(x_1))\cdots\gamma^{k_p}(\widetilde c_0(x_p))=(0,1).\]
Now by (6.6.4) and (6.5.3), this implies:
\[(k_1+\cdots+k_p)!\,c^{k_1}(x_1)\cdots c^{k_p}(x_p)=0.\]
Since \(K\) has characteristic zero, we have the desired result.
The universal property of \(\Omega\) applied to the canonical homomorphism \(\widetilde c\) gives a commutative diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{ \Lambda\ar[r]^{\widetilde c}\ar[d]_{\widetilde c_0}&{\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda)}\\ {\operatorname{Ch}(\Omega)}\ar[ur]& }\]

By Proposition 6.11, the homomorphism: \(\Omega\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda\) is an isomorphism if and only if the homomorphism: \(\operatorname{Ch}(\Omega)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda)\) is one. Since it is visibly surjective, the latter is as well, and it therefore suffices to show that \(\operatorname{Ch}(\Omega)\to\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda)\) is injective. By functoriality of \(\operatorname{Ch}\), we obtain the diagram:

\[ \xymatrix{ \Lambda\ar[r]^{\widetilde c_0}\ar[d]_{\widetilde c}&{\operatorname{Ch}(\Omega)}\ar[d]^{\widetilde c}\\ {\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda)}\ar[r]_{\operatorname{Ch}(\widetilde c_0)}&{\operatorname{Ch}(\operatorname{Gr}_\lambda(\operatorname{Ch}(\Omega)))} }\ ;\]

since \(\Omega\) has bounded degrees, the right-hand vertical arrow is an isomorphism (6.12), and using 6.11 we therefore obtain a morphism: \(\operatorname{Gr}\Lambda\longrightarrow\Omega\), the composite of the two being the identity by the universal property of \(\Omega\).
APPENDIX: The Adams operations \(\psi^k\)
We follow here a presentation due to J.P. Serre.

Let \(K\) be a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring. For every \(x\in K\), we denote by \(\frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_t(x))\) the derivative with respect to \(t\) of \(\lambda_t(x)\). We then define elements of \(K\), denoted \(\psi^k(x)\), by setting:
\[(7.1.1)\qquad \frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_t(x))/\lambda_t(x)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k-1}\psi^k(x)t^{k-1}.\]
The maps \(\psi^k:K\to K\) are additive; indeed, we have:

\[ \begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_t(x+y))/\lambda_t(x+y) &=\frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_t(x)\lambda_t(y))/\lambda_t(x)\lambda_t(y)\\ &=\frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_t(x))/\lambda_t(x)+\frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_t(y))/\lambda_t(y)\ ; \end{aligned}\]
and consequently, for every \(k\):
\[\psi^k(x+y)=\psi^k(x)+\psi^k(y).\]
By (7.1.1), the \(\psi^k\) are isobaric polynomials of weight \(k\) with respect to the \(\lambda^i\), with \(i=1,\ldots,k\). Let us give for example the values of \(\psi^1\) and \(\psi^2\):
\[ \begin{aligned} \psi^1(x)&=\lambda^1(x)=x,\\ \psi^2(x)&=x^2-2\lambda^2(x). \end{aligned}\]

Let \(K\) be a ring of characteristic 0; suppose given maps \(\psi^k\) from \(K\) to \(K\), additive, and such that \(\psi^1=\operatorname{Id}_K\). We then define a pre-\(\lambda\)-structure on \(K\) by setting:
\[(7.2.1)\qquad \lambda_t(x)=\exp\Bigl(\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k-1}\psi^k(x)t^k/k\Bigr).\]

Multiplicativity follows from the additivity of the \(\psi^k\). There is therefore an equivalence between the data of a pre-\(\lambda\)-structure on \(K\) and the data of the corresponding operations \(\psi^k\).

It is clear from formula (7.1.1) that the \(\psi^k\) commute with \(\lambda\)-homomorphisms. If \(K\), \(K'\) are pre-\(\lambda\)-rings of characteristic zero, it is necessary and sufficient that a homomorphism \(f:K\to K'\) commute with the \(\psi^k\) in order for it to be a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism: this follows from 7.2, the \(\lambda^i\) being expressible as polynomials in the \(\psi^k\).

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 7.4.

i) For \(K=\mathbb Z\), equipped with its \(\lambda\)-ring structure, we have:

\[\frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_t(n))/\lambda_t(n)=n(1+t)^{n-1}/(1+t)^n=n.\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k-1}t^{k-1}.\]

Consequently, the \(\psi^k\) are all equal to the identity map of \(\mathbb Z\).

ii) Let us place ourselves in the category \(\mathcal C_0\) of \(\mathbb Z\)-algebras (1.8). The operations \(\psi^k\) define, by 7.3, additive endomorphisms of the functor in \(\lambda\)-rings \(\widehat G\); by 1.10, they are characterized by their value at \(1+Xt\). We have:

\[ \begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_u(1+Xt))/\lambda_u(1+Xt) &=\frac{1+Xt}{1+\{1+Xt\}u}\\ &=\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k-1}\{1+Xt\}^ku^{k-1}. \end{aligned}\]

Consequently:

\[(7.4.1)\qquad\psi^k(1+Xt)=1+X^kt.\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.5.

i) If \(K\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring, the operations \(\psi^k\) satisfy the identities:

\[(7.5.1)\qquad \psi^k(1)=1\ ;\quad\psi^k(xy)=\psi^k(x)\psi^k(y)\ ;\quad\psi^k\circ\psi^{k'}=\psi^{kk'}.\]

ii) Conversely, if the \(\psi^t\) satisfy these identities, and if \(K\) has characteristic zero, then \(K\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring.
i) If \(K\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring, we have \(\lambda_t(1)=1+t\), whence:
\[\frac{d}{dt}(\lambda_t(1))/\lambda_t(1)=1/1+t=\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k-1}t^{k-1},\ \text{et par suite :}\]
\[\psi^k(1)=1.\]
To prove the second of the relations (7.5.1), we consider the diagram:

\[(7.5.2)\qquad \xymatrix{ K\times K\ar[r]^{\lambda_t\times\lambda_t}\ar[d]& \widehat G(K)\times\widehat G(K)\ar[r]^{\phi^k\times\phi^k}\ar[d]^{\circ}& K\times K\ar[d]\\ K\ar[r]_{\lambda_t}&\widehat G(K)\ar[r]_{\phi^k}&K, }\]

where the vertical arrows are defined by multiplication, and the maps \(\phi^k\) as follows: the "logarithmic derivative" map is a group homomorphism from \(\widehat G(K)\) to \(K[[t]]\); the map which to a series associates its \((k-1)\)-th coefficient, multiplied by \((-1)^{k-1}\), is an additive homomorphism from \(K[[t]]\) to \(K\); the morphism \(\phi^k\) will be the composite of the two. It is clear that \(\phi^k\circ\lambda_t=\psi^k\) by the definition (7.1.1).

The second of the formulas (7.5.1) therefore expresses the commutativity of the diagram written above. If we assume that \(K\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring, the left-hand square is commutative, and it suffices to verify the commutativity of the right-hand one. Now this is a diagram of homomorphisms of functors on \(\mathcal C_0\), with source \(\widehat G\times\widehat G\); it therefore suffices, by 1.10, to verify commutativity for \((1+Xt,1+Yt)\); we then have:

\[ \begin{aligned} &\phi^k(1+Xt)=X^k\ (\ (k-1)\text{-iĂ¨me coefficient de }X/1+Xt\ )\ ;\quad\phi^k(1+Yt)=Y^k\ ;\\ &\phi^k((1+Xt)\circ(1+Yt))=\phi^k(1+XYt)=(XY)^k=\phi^k(1+Xt)\phi^k(1+Yt), \end{aligned}\]
which gives the result.
To prove the last one, we first note that the diagram:
\[(7.5.3)\qquad \xymatrix{ \widehat G^t(K)\ar[r]^{\lambda_u}\ar[d]_{\phi^{kk'}}& \widehat G^u\circ\widehat G^t(K)\ar[d]^{\phi_u^{k'}}\\ K\ar[r]_{\phi^k}&\widehat G^t(K), }\]
is commutative. As above, it indeed suffices to see this for \(1+Xt\), and we have:
\[ \begin{aligned} \phi_u^{k'}(\lambda_u(1+Xt))&=\phi_u^{k'}(1+\{1+Xt\}u)=(1+Xt)^{\circ k'}=1+X^{k'}t\ ;\\ \phi^k(1+X^{k'}t)&=X^{kk'}=\phi^{kk'}(1+Xt). \end{aligned}\]
Consequently, \(\psi^{kk'}=\phi^k\circ\phi_u^{k'}\circ\lambda_u\circ\lambda_t=\phi^k\circ\psi^{k'}\circ\lambda_t\).
Moreover, \(\psi^k\circ\psi^{k'}=\phi^k\circ\lambda_t\circ\phi^{k'}\circ\lambda_t=\phi^k\circ\lambda_t\circ\psi^{k'}\).
Now if \(K\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring, the diagram:
\[(7.5.4)\qquad \xymatrix{ K\ar[r]^{\lambda_t}\ar[d]_{\psi^{k'}}&\widehat G(K)\ar[d]^{\psi^{k'}}\\ K\ar[r]_{\lambda_t}&\widehat G(K) }\]
is commutative by 7.3, since \(\lambda_t\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism. We immediately deduce the desired relations.

ii) If \(K\) has characteristic zero, the "logarithmic derivative" map is an isomorphism from \(\widehat G(K)\) onto \(K[[t]]\), since we can integrate. Consequently, two elements of \(\widehat G(K)\) are equal if, and only if, for every \(k\) they have the same image under \(\phi^k\).

To show that \(\lambda_t\) is a ring homomorphism, we must show the commutativity of the left-hand square of the diagram (7.5.2), and by the preceding remark it suffices to show that for every \(k\) the diagram (7.5.2) is commutative, since the right-hand square always is. Now this is none other than the relations \(\psi^k(xy)=\psi^k(x)\,\psi^k(y)\).

To show that \(\lambda_t\) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, it suffices to show that the diagram (7.5.4) is commutative for every \(k'\), by 7.3. Now, by the remark above, this amounts to showing, for all \(k\), \(k'\), that \(\phi^k\circ\psi^{k'}\circ\lambda_t=\phi^k\circ\lambda_t\circ\psi^{k'}\), that is, the relations \(\psi^{kk'}=\psi^k\circ\psi^{k'}\).
Finally, the relation \(\lambda_t(1)=1+t\) is obtained by integration from the relations \(\psi^k(1)=1\).
BIBLIOGRAPHY

[2] F. Hirzebruch, Neue topologische Methoden in der algebraischen Geometrie.
